Patrick Collison’s advice for people in their 20s: “Work at a place with high standards”
When asked for career advice for people in their 20s, Patrick recalls the book Apprentice to Genius. It follows three generations of scientists that mentored each other and were all extremely successful. It then reflects on what was transferred.
One important thing that the mentees say they learned from their mentors was problem selection. In science, you have to choose problems that are sufficiently important yet not so intractable that you can’t actually make any progress.
The other thing the mentees describe is just learning about high standards and what high standards actually are.
Patrick believes this is a really important point:
“When I talk to people in other domains, this is so frequently the thing that I hear from them. That when they worked with X person or Y organization or in Z environment, they learned what great actually is, and that just permanently changed their sense for what their own standard for their work ought to be.”
He continues:
“So maybe one versions of the ‘what people in their twenties should do’ is get some idea as to the domains you’re interested in, but then figure out where you can learn the highest standards. Where are the highest standards embodied? And where can you go and experience that firsthand?”

Why studying history makes you better at solving modern problems
Most careers are built around finding the right answer quickly. But what if the strength lies not in one answer, but in seeing all the possible ones?
That’s what a history degree forces you to do. You’re trained to hold multiple perspectives at once, to weigh conflicting evidence, and to make sense of messy, incomplete information. It’s not about memorising dates; it’s about learning to map the “why” behind events, and the “how” behind decisions. Those same skills carry over into today’s challenges: where no problem is ever straightforward, and every solution has trade-offs.
I’ve found this to be true in my own journey. The skills I built through studying history—critical analysis, pulling from different sources, and considering outcomes from every angle—have helped me navigate decisions far beyond the classroom. Whether it’s in projects, problem-solving, or strategy, that habit of asking “what else could be true?” often makes the difference.
The biggest takeaway? Careers rarely reward you for having a single neat answer. They reward you for being able to explore the full picture, connect the dots, and make thoughtful choices in uncertain situations.
Because in the end, the best problem-solvers aren’t the ones who know everything. They’re the ones who stay curious enough to keep asking what possibilities they might be missing.

Academia is full of opportunities —committees, reviews, lectures, panels, grants. Each one has value, but saying yes to everything risks exhausting both time and energy.
In this blog, Dr[Becky Carlyle](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/becky-carlyle-bb399118?trk=public_post-text)shares her perspective on the demands of an academic career. Drawing on her experience in both the US and UK, and lessons from elite sport, she explains how clear long-term goals and flexibility are essential for decision making.
She outlines how priorities shift with career stage, why mentors and networks are invaluable, and how learning to say no is not an apology but an act of self-preservation.
Becky's reflections highlight practical ways to stay on track, adapt to setbacks, and maintain ownership of your path.
Read or Listen to the full blog here:[https://lnkd.in/djHByyw2](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/djHByyw2&urlhash=Y3E2&trk=public_post-text)

There’s a big conversation right now around how our education system prepares (or rather fails to prepare) students for the workforce. So I started reflecting: What undergraduate class best prepared me for my current job as a management consultant? The answer was surprising. It wasn’t any of my Econ classes (though they are up there) and it certainly wasn’t any of my business classes (finance was probably the only useful one). If you want to read about why, check out the longer post in my recently launched Substack publication, Learning Well, where I’m exploring trends and fallacies in how we talk about education, seeking to uncover the truth about how humans learn, and reflecting on what it all means for how we approach school, careers, and personal well-being.

Beyond the Grade: Why Reading is Your Unfair Advantage in the Modern Workforce. We've all been there: cramming for an exam, memorizing facts just long enough to secure a passing grade, and then promptly forgetting them. The education system often incentivizes short-term recall over deep, lasting understanding. It's a game, and many of us have learned to play it well.
But here's the reality check the syllabus won't give you: that game ends at graduation.
When you land that dream job, no one will hand you a past paper to memorize. They will hand you a complex problem, a client file, a line of code, a financial report, or a patient case study and say, "What do we do?"
This is where the true value of consistent, engaged reading reveals itself. It's not about passing a test; it's about building a robust mental framework.
·Reading builds competence, not just credentials. Your degree gets you the interview; your knowledge gets the work done. You can't "fake it till you make it" when a project's success depends on your foundational understanding of the field.
·It fosters critical thinking. Books and detailed articles present nuanced arguments and complex systems. This trains your brain to analyze, synthesize, and innovate—skills that are irreplaceable and highly valued.
·It prepares you for the unexpected. The real world is messy and doesn't follow a textbook's chapter outline. A broad knowledge base, built through reading, allows you to adapt and apply concepts to novel situations.
Don't just read to pass. Read to prepare. Your future self—and your future employer—will thank you for the depth you cultivated when no one was awarding points for it.
What's one book that fundamentally changed your understanding of your field? ✨Every question opens a new perspective.
Questions are not just words –they are the keys to curiosity, learning, and meaningful conversations. From What and Why to Do and Is, every question helps us understand ourselves and others better.
Here’s a set of thoughtful questions you can use to spark a conversation about learning, interests, and passions 👇🔹What is your favourite subject?
🔹Why do you like it?
🔹Where did you first learn about it?
🔹When did you start liking it?
🔹Which topic in it excites you most?
🔹Whom do you discuss it with?
🔹With whom do you study it?
🔹How do you prepare for it?
🔹How much time do you spend on it?
🔹How many books do you read for it?
🔹How often do you practice it?
🔹How long have you been interested in it?
🔹Since when have you studied it seriously?
🔹Can you explain why you love it?
🔹Could you tell me who inspired you?
🔹Would you like to make a career in it?
🔹May I know who teaches it?
🔹Where do you study it best?
🔹Do you enjoy solving problems in it?
🔹Did you always like it since childhood?
🔹Does it help you in daily life?
🔹Have you ever scored full marks in it?
🔹Has it inspired you to try something new?
🔹Had you liked it before this school/college?
🔹Was it your favourite in earlier classes too?
🔹Is it useful for your future career?
🔹Who is your favourite teacher for it?
🔹Are you planning to study it further?
💡Questions open doors. The more we ask, the deeper we understand.

A group of successful graduates once visited their former professor.
They had built impressive careers, held prestigious titles, and lived seemingly perfect lives.
While waiting for coffee, their conversation turned to the stresses of work, exhaustion, and the pressures of adulthood.
Some laughed.
Some complained.
Some philosophized.
The professor returned with a tray full of mismatched cups —porcelain and glass, ceramic and plastic, elegant and chipped, minimalist and ornate.
As the guests chose their cups, the professor quietly observed…
Then he spoke:
“Notice how you all instinctively reached for the finest cups —the fancy ones.
Not a single person chose the worn-out or plastic ones.
And that’s the root of much of your stress.”
“You wanted the coffee —not the cup.
But you focused on the cup’s appearance, not the essence of what you came for.”
He continued:
“Life is like coffee.
Career, salary, status, home, car —those are just cups.
They don’t define the richness of life itself.”
“Sometimes we get so distracted by the ‘cup’,
we forget to savor the coffee.”
☕So drink your coffee with joy.
Not by comparing.
Not by competing.
Not by glancing sideways.
Because the happiest people aren’t those who have the best of everything —but those who know how to make the best of what they have.

A group of successful graduates once visited their former professor.
They had built impressive careers, held prestigious titles, and lived seemingly perfect lives.
While waiting for coffee, their conversation turned to the stresses of work, exhaustion, and the pressures of adulthood.
Some laughed.
Some complained.
Some philosophized.
The professor returned with a tray full of mismatched cups —porcelain and glass, ceramic and plastic, elegant and chipped, minimalist and ornate.
As the guests chose their cups, the professor quietly observed…
Then he spoke:
“Notice how you all instinctively reached for the finest cups —the fancy ones.
Not a single person chose the worn-out or plastic ones.
And that’s the root of much of your stress.”
“You wanted the coffee —not the cup.
But you focused on the cup’s appearance, not the essence of what you came for.”
He continued:
“Life is like coffee.
Career, salary, status, home, car —those are just cups.
They don’t define the richness of life itself.”
“Sometimes we get so distracted by the ‘cup’,
we forget to savor the coffee.”
☕So drink your coffee with joy.
Not by comparing.
Not by competing.
Not by glancing sideways.
Because the happiest people aren’t those who have the best of everything —but those who know how to make the best of what they have.

A Story from my college experience That Will Hit Different 🎓:-
Scene: 2 AM, hostel room, coffee cup Me (to my roommate):"Dude, I'm literally failing. Engineering is impossible. I've been studying for 12 hours straight!"
Roommate: "Bro, you've been scrolling reels for 11 of those hours."
Me:"...fair point."
Flashback to 3 days ago when I said I'd start studying "tomorrow"
The Reality Check:
I realized I wasn't the struggling genius I thought I was. I was just really good at making excuses and really bad at time management.
The Turning Point:
That night, I discovered something that changed everything...
The Study Hacks That Actually Worked:
The "Fake Deadline" Trick: I started telling myself exams were 2 days earlier. Suddenly, I had time to actually understand concepts instead of just memorizing.
Phone Jail: Literally put my phone in another room. The withdrawal was real, but so were the results.
The 5-Minute Rule: If I could explain a concept in 5 minutes to someone who knows nothing about it, I actually understood it.
Past Paper Goldmine: 90% of my "studying" became solving past papers. Professors are creatures of habit.
Caffeine + Movement: 25 minutes study, 5 minutes walking around the room. My grades went up, my sanity stayed intact.
The Plot Twist:
Turns out, engineering wasn't the villain in my story—my procrastination was.
The Happy Ending:
I went from "barely passing" to "actually understanding what I'm doing." And yes, I still cram sometimes, but now it's strategic cramming.
Now it's your turn to share:
What's your most embarrassing study confession?
- The time you "studied" for 8 hours but actually just reorganized your desk?
- When you realized the exam was tomorrow, not next week?
- That moment you understood a concept 5 minutes before the exam?
Drop your story below! Patrick Collison: “Working with people you’re close to is underrated.”
Patrick started Stripe with his brother John and Arc Institute with his wife Silvana. He offers founders the following advice:
“I think working with people you’re close to is underrated… For all of the ventures of any significance in my life, they’ve been with people that I’m very close to and who I had and would like to have an enduring relationship with. Sometimes one hears the advice that you shouldn’t work with friends or your partner, and all of these things are idiosyncratic and there are instances of every possible permutation, but for me it has been a really rewarding experience.”
He continues:
“I think John and I can work together for, you never know, life. But I think we’ll probably work together for decades. For us, it has been both an important source of meaning and fulfillment, but I also think there’s a real complementarity.”

# Mika Tikkala’s Post
Mika Tikkala
1y
-
Patrick Collison’s Leadership Turned Stripe Into a $95B Payments Juggernaut. I spent weeks breaking down his interviews, culture docs, and growth playbooks. The result? A masterclass in leadership. Here’s how Patrick’s philosophy turned Stripe into a global payments backbone (and how you can apply it): 1. “Think 100 Years Ahead” (But Execute Tomorrow) Patrick famously tells employees: “Assume Stripe will exist for 100+ years. Now, what’s worth doing today?” How this plays out: Relentless focus on infrastructure: While competitors chased quick wins, Stripe spent years building global compliance systems (now used by 150+ countries). No “growth at all costs”: In 2023, Stripe delayed its IPO to avoid “market noise” and doubled down on profitability. Balance audacious vision with ruthless prioritization. Ask: “Will this decision matter in 5 years?” 2. The “Engineer as CEO” MentalityPatrick (a self-taught coder) leads like a systems architect — scaling culture through principles, not policies. Example: Stripe’s “Write Everything Down” Rule Every major decision is documented in Canonical (Stripe’s internal wiki) Engineers write “bug post-mortems” — Patrick applies this to leadership, publishing “decision autopsies” on failed bets. Why it works: Transparency eliminates tribal knowledge. New hires onboard faster, and missteps become team learnings. 3. The “Talent Density” Obsession Stripe’s hiring bar is Silicon Valley legend. But Patrick’s secret isn’t just hiring A-players — it’s keeping them obsessed. Tactics to steal: “Pre-shipping” projects: Engineers pitch ideas before joining (e.g., a candidate built Stripe’s fraud detection prototype during interviews). No managers for the first 100 hires: Patrick believed early autonomy would attract “self-directed killers.” It worked — Stripe’s first 10 engineers stayed 8+ years. 4. “Default to Open” CommunicationPatrick banned “closed-door strategy sessions” in 2015. How Stripe shares everything: Monthly “Ask Me Anything” docs: Patrick answers 100+ raw questions from employees (e.g., “Why did we cut X feature?”). Customer emails forwarded company-wide: Every complaint/praise is visible, forcing teams to confront blind spots. Result: Stripe’s Glassdoor rating (4.7) beats Google, and attrition is 40% below industry average. 5. The “Arbitrage Learning” Hack Patrick’s reading list for execs (e.g., “Why Nations Fail”) isn’t for show — it’s strategy fuel. His rule: If you solve a problem, share how you learned to solve it. When Stripe struggled with remote work, Patrick studied NASA’s Mars mission team structures. Before expanding to Africa, he spent 3 months interviewing 200+ local founders. Stripe’s $95B valuation isn’t about payments… it’s about leadership that scales thinking, not just headcount. Want to build a company that outlasts market cycles? Follow for regular breakdowns of category-defining CEOs.

Why Software Companies are leaving Stripe: Stripe made payments simple. That’s why everyone started there. But Stripe was built for companies that just need to process transactions, not the ones that want to actually make money on them. When you’re doing real volume, that 2.9 percent plus 30 cents per transaction adds up fast. Unless you’re marking it up, you’re not earning anything from payments. You’re just handing margin to Stripe. That’s why more software companies are rethinking it. They’re realizing that if they already manage the customer, the billing, and the platform, there’s no reason they shouldn’t take a piece of the payment flow too. It’s not about hating Stripe. It’s about outgrowing it. At a certain point, it makes more sense to own the payment layer, keep control of the experience, and share in the margin instead of giving it all away. Stripe is perfect for starting out. Embedded payments are what make sense once you grow up.

The 7 lines of code that turned Stripe into a $95B company (while others failed) In 2010, accepting online payments was so complex it required: • 3+ weeks of engineering time • Multiple bank approvals • Security compliance headaches • Custom integration work Dozens of payment startups were adding features, creating "full-solution platforms" to solve this. But Patrick and John Collison made the opposite decision: They created a payment API requiring just 7 lines of code. When customers asked for features, they refused and doubled down on simplicity. Their "7-line" strategy seemed crazy but created 3 advantages: 1. Developer virality: Engineers shared Stripe because it was "beautiful code" — giving them free distribution when competitors spent millions on sales 2. Integration moat: By controlling the full payment stack (not outsourcing), they delivered 99.99% uptime while competitors suffered outages 3. Enterprise foundation: The simple API actually scaled better for large customers, enabling Stripe to move upmarket while feature-focused competitors got stuck downmarket The results: • 2010: 7-line MVP • 2011: $2M in funding • 2016: Processing billions monthly • 2021: $95B valuation • 2024: Powers >50% of online businesses Meanwhile, dozens of well-funded competitors with "more features" failed. Key founder lesson: Adding features feels like progress but often destroys it. The most valuable startups don't win by doing more things — they win by doing one critical thing 10X better. What's the one core function your product needs to nail before anything else?

Wondering why new features at Stripe take weeks – and at legacy players, still years? The difference isn't team size or resources. It's architectural debt versus architectural advantage. Legacy payment providers built monolithic systems when online payments were simple. Every new feature requires untangling years of interdependent code, extensive testing across rigid integrations, and complex deployment cycles. What should take weeks stretches into quarters or years. Modern composable architectures flip this entirely. Instead of one massive system doing everything, you get modular services that connect flexibly. Need a new payment method? Plug it in. Want to adjust routing logic? Configure, don't rebuild. Developer-friendly APIs replace those painful monolithic integrations that require months of planning. This is exactly why we built Hellgate®'s Composable Payment Architecture (CPA) as our foundation. CPA isn't just about technical elegance – it's about merchant success. When you can deliver new capabilities in weeks instead of years, you're not just keeping up with market demands, you're staying ahead of them. Your conversion rates don't wait for lengthy development cycles. Your customers don't pause their expectations while you untangle legacy code. In payments, speed to market directly impacts revenue. The enterprises winning in today's market aren't just processing transactions – they're adapting faster than their competition. Composable beats complexity. CPA is the architecture advantage of the future. 🚀

Everyone keeps trying to replace Stripe. Then they realize why no one can. Every few months, someone announces they’re building a “Stripe killer.” Then a year later, they quietly migrate back. It’s easy to copy a payment form. It’s impossible to copy an ecosystem. Stripe built more than an API, it built gravity. Payments, billing, stablecoins, treasury, usage tracking, everything flows toward it. Founders complain about fees or bugs, but their invoices, payouts, and customer data all still sit inside Stripe. Pull one thread and the whole stack moves with it. That’s why Stripe never really loses customers. It just waits for them to realize they never left.

And that’s what makes it so expensive: → 2% of payments failing quietly every month → Only 20–30% of those getting recovered by default with Stripe → ~1.5% of MRR disappearing without a sound For his $12M ARR company? That’s $18k/month in silent churn Just gone. But it’s not just MRR. That’s a full-time engineer. Or 3 quarters of paid acquisition. Or a buffer that buys you more optionality next fundraising cycle. Revenue isn’t the only thing leaking. Momentum is too. If you’ve got more than $15k/month in failed payments and want to see exactly where you’re leaking and how to fix it, I’ll show you. Free audit. Just your numbers, your loss, and what to do about it. You’ll get a full breakdown of your results and a strategy you can take back to your team. Drop a comment and I’ll take a look.

Patrick Collison on the importance of waiting a really long time to hire people
Everybody tells you “work really hard to hire the best people.” But as Patrick explains, that’s not helpful because everyone knows they should do that. As he puts it:
“The question is to what length should you go and what does that actually mean in practice? And in practice, it means being okay waiting a really long time to hire people.”
It took Stripe six months to hire their first two people, and six more months to hire another 3-4 people. He can think of five people at Stripe who took 3+ years to hire.
“If you think about the smartest people you know, if you want to get them to work on your thing, chances are they already have pretty good paths ahead of them… You have to be way more persistent and be okay with it taking way longer than any sane or reasonable person would think it should take.”
There’s an important compounding effect here —hiring just one great person will make it marginally easier to get the next great person. Patrick argues you should also view every person you hire as bringing along another 50 people just like them if your company is successful:
“Even if they don’t literally hire 50 people, they will be so influential in determining the selection of those 50 people.”
Video source:[Greylock](https://www.linkedin.com/company/greylock-partners?trk=public_post-text)(2015)

TranscriptTranscript
##### **Transcript**
Received wisdom is worked really hard to hire the best people and you're like, Gee, thanks. You know, I was going to do the opposite. But now you've said that work really hard to hire the best people. And so I guess the question is sort of what length should you go and kind of what does it actually mean in practice? And in practice it means being OK waiting a really long time to hire people. It took me 6 months to hire the first two people, so a person per quarter, even though we had this kind of network already and you all these people, we've managed like one every three months and then in I think the six months after that. We may be hired another. Three or four people kind of on that order, yeah. You just think about of the smartest people you know and you want to get them to work on your thing. I mean, chances are as far as people you know are working already, yeah, they like they have like pretty good paths out of them, right? There were multiple people that stripe now today who took us several years to hire. I can think off the top of my head of maybe five people who took three plus years to hire. The big thing that people have to keep in mind instead of early stage company recruiting is just you have to be way more persistent. To be OK with taking way longer than any sane or reasonable person you know could think it should take. The vicious cycle is that you're going so slowly when it's the first two or three people and you're like, holy smokes, if we could just get another set of hands and you know, but you don't want to do it. But we talk about this class about compound interest and the value of compounding interest. Like if you get just one great person, right, that makes it marginally easier to get the next great person. So I think that dynamic in spades. And then kind of secondly, I think another way to think about it makes the kind of the importance of it and the potency. That tier is that you have to kind of imagine the tree of sort of all the people this person will hire and going rather than just think about it as kind of do do I want this person or not? It's like, do I want this person and the 50 people who I think they will hire? Because even if they don't literally hire 50 people, they will be kind of so influential in determining sort of the self selection of those 50 people.

We have this joke at Leap - "Speed as a habit - in everything except hiring"
We want to hire slow
As we grow older and wiser as a company, we are getting slower in hiring
The speed of hiring is inversely correlated with the quality of hires you make
It is absolutely okay to wait a really really long time to hire the right person.
And the worst hiring mistakes we have made are the ones where the hiring manager was tired of searching for 2 months and hired the first passably good candidate out of exhaustion and desperation.
Classical sunk cost and local maxima optimisation scenario
Hire slow
Never hire out of exhaustion
Hire Bar-Raisers
Wait for a long time for the right hire

🚀The Hidden Superpower of Great Hiring: Patience 🕰️Everyone says, "Hire the best people." But as[Stripe](https://www.linkedin.com/company/stripe?trk=public_post-text)'s[Patrick Collison](https://www.linkedin.com/in/patrickcollison?trk=public_post-text)highlights, that's not the real insight. The game-changer? Understanding what it truly takes to hire the best. And spoiler: it’s a lot more patience than you think.
Here’s Patrick’s perspective:
💡“The question is to what length should you go and what does that actually mean in practice? In practice, it means being okay waiting a really long time to hire people.”
💼At Stripe, it took 6 months to hire their first two employees. Another 6 months to find the next 3-4. Patrick even recalls 5 hires that took over 3 years of persistence to land.
Why? Because the smartest, most capable people often have great opportunities lined up. To bring them into your vision, you have to go further than most would —more persistence, more patience, more long-term thinking.
But here’s the payoff: Compounding talent.
Every great hire doesn’t just add value. They become a magnet for more greatness. Patrick says:
💬“Even if they don’t literally hire 50 people, they will be so influential in determining the selection of those 50 people.”
🔥Takeaway: Building an extraordinary team isn’t about speed; it’s about compounding excellence through relentless patience and unwavering vision.

3mo
I'm convinced that most tech companies are massively overstaffed. Yet the recent announcements of multiple tech companies to fire 5% of "low-performers" ultimately get it backward.
Overstaffing needs to be fixed with better (and less) hiring and not "better" firing!
Firing 5% of "low-performers" means a) you need to come up with metrics for rating your employees which leads to b) your workforce focusing on gaming those metrics (Goodhart's law).
And don't get me started on natural performance fluctuations that happen in the life of humans (e.g., because of the death of a loved one, break-ups, childbirth,...).
Hire when it hurts, and fire when it really hurts, but not sooner!

Executive Director @ Truggl.video | Animated sales videos to sell | B2B SaaS, Fintech, Legal, HealthTech
5moEdited
We experienced a spike in revenue from the middle of August until today.
Funny enough, if you open our Stripe, it's the first thing you'll notice. 😂How did that happen?
I put arrogance aside and turned it off.
I started receiving a lot of feedback. And when I say a lot, I mean an annoyingly large amount. I ask clients about everything where, when, and how and I record those conversations to share with my team.
And if you think it stopped there, no, it didn't. It went way further.
I know some great people—really great entrepreneurs and marketing experts.
Some of them were my clients, some were friends, and some were just acquaintances.
I actually get on calls or meet with them, telling them everything imaginable about my business:
- Where we are at
- What we are planning to do
- How we are planning to do it
I genuinely enjoy listening to their feedback and taking notes.
Some of them said, "Then why aren't you recording content?"
So, now I record content four times a month.
Some suggested, "Why don't you hire more people?"
So, I started hiring like crazy.
Others even pointed out, "You have a problem in your system—why do you make clients wait for a quote?"
As a result, we added a pricing page at[truggl.com/pricing](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http://truggl.com/pricing&urlhash=62C3&trk=public_post-text).
I always take that feedback positively, even if it feels offensive, and use it to grow my business.
I even get messages from young people asking how I do what I do. I tell them it’s simple: you're young, inexperienced, and have nothing to lose.
Open your laptop, learn something, post content every day even if you see no immediate results, sell your services, and congratulations—you’re on the path I took.
(And when I say 'kids,' I mean people my age 😅).
---[truggl.video](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=http://truggl.video&urlhash=LlZs&trk=public_post-text)for great videos.

# Fahim Sachedina’s Post
[![View profile for Fahim Sachedina]()](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/fahimsach?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)

2mo
Patrick Collison's (co-founder & CEO at[Stripe](https://www.linkedin.com/company/stripe?trk=public_post-text)) advice for people in their 20's: “𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗲𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗵𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗮𝗿𝗱𝘀”Looking back, the times I learned the most had one thing in common: 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲𝗜𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗲𝗱𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵.
Not just their intelligence, but:
→Did they challenge me?
→Did they push me to grow?
→Did they create an environment where excellence was the norm?
The higher the standards around me, the higher my own baseline became. Those experiences rewired my expectations of what "great work" really looks like.
As Patrick says: “One way to think about your 20s is to explore different domains—but more importantly, find the places where the highest standards exist. Where can you go to experience excellence firsthand?”
Where have you worked that raised your personal standards the most?

##### **Transcript**
There's interesting book Apprentice to Genius that follows or it's 3 or 4. It's 3 generations of scientists. So you know, someone who mentored somebody else who in turn mentored another scientist. And they're all extremely successful. And the book is kind of this, this, this reflection on, I mean, this, this description of how they all did, but also this kind of reflection about like, what is it that was transferred? And you know, for example, one thing it describes is well. And the most important and subtle, you know, questions in sciences, problem selection, like just how do you choose what to work on, right? No, no, no one tells you what to do. And, and, and you do have to answer this question multiple times, like with the company, in some sense, you have to decide like once and then, you know, it's kind of, well, maybe it's an iterative process from there was in science you're, you're frequently pursuing, you know, completely new problems. And of course you choose something that's, you know, sufficiently important and hard that would be important if you succeeded, but also that. That, you know, it's not so intractable that, you know, you can't actually make any progress. And so the the book describes how this is part of what the mentees described, that, you know, they learned from their mentors. Another thing they talk about is just learning about high standards and what high standards actually are. And when I talk to people in other domains, this is so frequently the thing that I hear from them that when they worked with X person or. Why your organization or in the environment or whatever that they learned what great actually is and that just permanently changed their sense for what their what their own standard for their work ought to be and so. And so maybe one version of the, you know, people in the 20s should do is is, you know. Get get some ideas to domain you're interested in or care about, but then figure out where can you learn the highest standards, Where are the highest standards embodied and where you know where where where can you go and experience that first hand.

# Stripe announces new products and features for payments and commerce
This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
11mo
The Stripe product keynote just finished! We announced a lot of new stuff. (We actually couldn't fit everything into the keynote.) Some highlights: • We've built and deployed the first general-purpose Payments Foundation Model. Trained on tens of billions of transactions and already leading to very large fraud reductions. • We announced Stablecoin Financial Accounts. Businesses in 101(!) more countries can now store stablecoin balances in Stripe and use our regular fiat money movement rails around the world. The magic of stablecoins comes when they're integrated with the rest of the financial ecosystem. I think it's the biggest international launch we'll ever do -- businesses from 151 countries can now use Stripe. • Stripe Profiles: your public identity on the Stripe network. Stripe Profiles will let you streamline invoicing and payments with other businesses on Stripe. (We’ll soon launch reservations for Stripe Profiles.) • Stripe Verified, a new service that will help you navigate global requirements for payment processing with personalized monitoring, enhanced protections, and expert assistance. • We're adding multicurrency support to Stripe accounts: instead of converting all balances to a single currency, we're going to make it easy to store, convert, and operate across different currencies. We want to make it possible to use Stripe as your financial home. • Stripe Orchestration: manage multiple payment processors directly within Stripe. • Support for many new payment methods. You can now access more than 125 global payment methods on Stripe, including stablecoins and real-time payment schemes like Pix and UPI. • We’ve upgraded the AI models built into Optimized Checkout Suite and now use more than 100 signals to personalize the checkout in real time. A/B tests indicate that Optimized Checkout Suite increases revenue by 11.9% on average. • Managed Payments: merchant of record is coming to Stripe! Enter new markets while we take care of global taxes, fraud prevention, dispute management, fulfillment, and more. • A new Connect dashboard, with vastly improved visibility into what's happening across your platform. • Radar for Connect: it’s now way easier to detect and manage fraud and compliance issues with subaccounts. • Networked onboarding for platforms: users no longer need to re-enter their details if they’ve onboarded before. • Smart Disputes: AI automatically responds to disputes for you. Early results show that companies win 13% more chargebacks. We also put skin in the game: we cover the fee if you lose. • Radar now supports ACH and SEPA. This reduces fraud rates by 20% and 42% respectively. • A bunch of usage-based billing improvements. It can now ingest up to 100,000 events every second. Rest (there are many) available in my Tweet: https://lnkd.in/gZS5yugT. The full list of launches is beyond the LinkedIn character limit.

The move toward multicurrency support, stablecoin integration, and orchestration tools shows a clear understanding of where modern finance is headed. And the application of AI for fraud, disputes, and checkout optimization? That’s the kind of intelligence that drives real business outcomes. Excited to see how these launches ripple across the ecosystem.

I often use the fact that we are a young, smaller company (with plenty of payment nerds) as an advantage for our customers. Let me give you a concrete example from just a few weeks ago: A customer reported an occasional issue: after some voids, funds stayed “stuck.” The root cause was that reversals weren’t always pointing back to the correct authorization. When that happened, the issuer’s systems couldn’t match things up, and the reversal failed. The result: customers left waiting, issuers confused, and support teams dealing with queues. Here’s how we approached it:We dug into the traces with the tech teams of our partners and several issuers (shoutout to the founders of some neobanks who were extremely cooperative), comparing how reference IDs were being passed. Our side: We quickly shipped a change so our reversals would always tie back to the right transaction, regardless of downstream issues—eliminating ambiguity. Their side: Our partner confirmed the bug on their end and committed to a long-term patch. The issuer also improved how messages were being interpreted. Together: By aligning both fixes, we created a solution that now works across the entire chain. The issue has since been fixed. Issuers get better visibility, support queues are easing, and customers enjoy a smoother experience. It wasn’t just about solving a bug. It was about owning our share of the problem, working across teams, and delivering something better than what we started with. This is our advantage Embed!

Patrick Collison on what most startups get wrong pre-product/market fit “The main thing that I think companies screw up at the pre-product/market fit stage is speed of iteration. If you have some kind of meaningful—albeit perhaps small—initial set of users and you’re rapidly iterating in response to their feedback and observed behavior, I think that’s a really good spot to be in… You should be doing everything you can to tighten that feedback cycle.” Patrick believes most startups get this wrong when it comes to hiring — either they hire the wrong people or they hire too many people. Hiring isn’t just expensive financially, but it often makes you move slower. You have to onboard them, get them up to speed, and then there’s a persistent communication and alignment tax that scales quadratically with more team members. Patrick believes the ultimate arbiter for a new hire should be, “Will we be more responsive to what we’re learning about our users given the presence of this additional person?” Empirically the “optimally responsive” team for a pre-product/market fit startup seems to be 2-10 people who are all either building the product or talking to users. Video source: Y Combinator(2018)

Every founder begins with a dream: the idea that their startup will be the one. But the cold reality? 90% of startups fail within five years. Meet “GreenBox,” a food-tech startup that launched with great excitement. Investors poured in, the media hyped them, and customers initially loved their eco-packaging solutions. Yet, within 24 months, they shut down. Why? Four classic startup killers: ⚠️ No Market Validation They built what they thought people wanted instead of asking customers. Turns out, their main buyers didn’t see enough value. 💸 Cash Flow Mismanagement They spent like unicorns before becoming one. Ad budgets burned faster than revenue could refill. 👥 Weak Team Alignment Founders disagreed on direction, employees followed different goals, and chaos spread. 🚫 No Tech Strategy Their website crashed during peak sales. Scaling was an afterthought, not a plan. But here’s the secret no one tells you: failure is optional. Sam Altman's Startup Playbook asks: "Why do founders want to start this particular company?" Then: "How will the company one day be a monopoly?" His answer: only founders with a deep personal mission survive long enough to find out. Startups require "consistent intense effort" for years - harder than most founders could ever imagine. If you're talented enough to build a company, you could be a highly-paid employee with infinitely less stress. Mission is what makes the irrational choice make sense. OpenAI proves this. No commercial product until 2020. Realized they needed billions for compute with no revenue path. Before becoming inevitable, employees could have joined hotter companies with actual products. They didn't. Their mission (ensure AGI benefits all of humanity) wasn't a recruiting pitch. It was why those specific people couldn't work on anything else. Altman's test: if your idea doesn't "excite at least some people the first time they hear it, that's bad." Most founders work backwards - find an opportunity, craft a mission statement. The founders who reach monopoly-scale work forwards. They start with something they must build, then figure out how to make it massive. "A thousand people have every great idea. One becomes successful. The difference comes down to execution." Execution takes years of irrational persistence. Only mission can sustain that. P.S. Read Altman's Startup Playbook (link in comments)

Just real work that compounds. Startups don’t die from lack of strategy. They die from lack of execution. Fewer people doing the right work > more people talking about the work. Ask yourself: Are you optimizing for alignment… or actual progress? Start with action. Momentum follows. What’s the one thing you could stop overthinking and just ship this week?

For tech founders, there are four types of knowledge that are critical: 💻 Technical Knowledge: how to build the thing 💵 Go to Market Knowledge: how to sell the thing 🎓 Subject Matter Expertise: all the things that impact the thing 🗺️ Product Knowledge: how to vision the thing as a finished product, but also in all the iterations of its intermediate stages When I see an early-stage startup with all of these bases covered, I know it’s going to execute well. The startup doesn’t have to have all of this knowledge in-house, but they have trusted experts who can act decisively and with experience: advisors, partners, co-founders, executives in residence. On the other hand, when I see startups struggling, it’s because the team lacks one of these types of knowledge. They’re waiting until after the MVP to bring in the product person, or they’re trying to capitalize on their traction with vibecoding, or they believe good products sell themselves (they don’t). Or they’re trying to backfill knowledge gaps with constant LLM queries that lack the context, experience, and moral compass for reliable decision-making. You can get by for a little while without one of these, but not for long. Any serious early-stage founder should be looking to line up a knowledge source for each of these areas as part of their growth strategy. All four types of knowledge don’t have to live in one person, or even on payroll. But all four perspectives should influence how you approach and execute on your vision.#startups#founders#earlystage#tech

Startups are product-driven – success means market fit and user growth. 📈 Scalability: Agencies scale linearly, limited by manpower and billable hours. Most startups don’t fail because of competitors. They fail because of avoidable mistakes. After working with dozens of early-stage teams, here are 10 mistakes I’ve seen silently kill growth: 1. Launching into a market with no demand. Being “first” isn’t an advantage if nobody wants the product. 2. Building features without speaking to customers. 3. Waiting too long to sell. Sales should start the moment the MVP is live. 4. Leading by fear. Talented employees always have options. 5. Promising equity without putting it in writing. 6. Giving up too much equity early and losing control before traction. 7. Chasing hype instead of monetization. 8. Burning cash without tracking LTV, CAC, or runway. 9. Spending months on perfection instead of iterating quickly. 10. Ignoring competitors and assuming you’re the only one in the market. The pattern? Startups rarely die from external pressure, they die from internal blind spots. Which of these mistakes have you seen sink a business?

86 percent of authors don't know this basic truth… Books need hooks. Even if they're made-up statistics. A hook does exactly what it says. It catches the reader's attention at the start of the book and again the start of each chapter. It tells them what they're going to read about and why they should be interested. Hooks come in many forms. 🎣 An anecdote that makes the reader want to know what happens next: "When my team leader called me into her office, I had no idea that the meeting would change my life." 🎣 A striking quotation that makes the reader's curious about how it fits your argument: "I find that the harder I work, the more luck I seem to have." Thomas Jefferson's words should be written on every founder's wall… 🎣 A remarkable statistic that takes the reader by surprise: "86 percent of authors don't know this basic trick." (Tip: This works better if the statistic is real.) 🎣 A controversial statement that confirms or challenges the reader's ideas: "Being miserable is part of being an entrepreneur. Get used to it." 🎣 A random fact that stops the reader in their tracks: "In 2010 a British goldsmith engraved the Lord's Prayer on the head of a pin." The hook needs to be long enough to do three things: • Get the reader's attention • Introduce the rest of the chapter • Get out of the way and let the chapter start Finding a hook isn't always easy … but once you find it, you're halfway to planning a great chapter. Book Recommendation 📚 I read The Psychology of Money by Morgan Housel a few months ago and it’s one of those books that’s hard to forget. What makes it stand out is that it’s not wholly a book about finance… it’s a book about people. How our experiences, emotions, and even luck shape the way we think about money. One part that really stayed with me is the idea that your personal history shapes your financial beliefs. Someone who grew up during a recession will likely view risk completely differently to someone raised in a bull market. Neither is wrong, they’re just coming from different worlds. As Housel puts it: “Your personal experiences with money make up maybe 0.00000001% of what’s happened in the world, but maybe 80% of how you think the world works.” That perspective made me step back and realise: money isn’t purely logical. It’s deeply personal. If you haven’t read it yet, I’d highly recommend it. It’s one of the most refreshing takes on money I’ve come across. (And no, this isn’t a paid review… but Morgan, if you’re reading, I wouldn’t say no!)

Scaling Stripe with Patrick Collison — Class 11 Notes of Stanford University’s CS183C
Agree & Join LinkedIn
` `` `` `` `` `` `` `
` `` `` `` `` `` `` `
` `` `` `` `
Here is an essay version of my class notes from Class 11 of Stanford University’s CS183C — Technology-enabled Blitzscaling— taught by Reid Hoffman, John Lilly, Chris Yeh, and Allen Blue. Errors and omissions are my own. Credit for good stuff is Reid, John, and Allen’s entirely.
This class was an interview by John Lilly, of Patrick Collison— the founder and CEO of Stripe — on lessons learned from scaling Stripe.
John Lilly: What is the founding story of Stripe?
Patrick Collison: I am skeptical about the “founding mythology” but I can share the story of how we started Stripe.
I started Stripe with my brother John Collison while we were in school together. We first started off building iPhone apps together and using the money we made from them to pay our tuition.
We got into the habit of working on side projects together early and realized it was easy to make money on iPhone apps (this was in the early days of the app store) and one of the things which made it so easy was the ability to charge and pay for things easily.
We were talking about why it was so much more difficult to charge for things online vs. on the app store — most websites made it a huge pain to pay for things. At the time there was this company called Slicehost which was the first really good virtual hosting provider — it made getting a server very straightforward. Our original idea was why don’t we build Slicehost for payments.
Originally we were just going to build a prototype and assumed it wouldn't be too hard. 5+ years later we are still at it — you can call it the world’s longest “ yak shave.”
In 2010, we built and released the first version of Stripe — actually, back then it was named /dev/payments (we weren’t great branding experts). We launched it to a few friends to get enough of a sense for the basic experience of charging for credit cards. We just put up a wait list on our site and demand took off, over a couple months we had a huge wait list. We weren’t expecting it.
John Lilly: You created the first version and then how did people find out about it? What kinds of people were signing up?
Patrick Collison: I had been involved in the LISP programming community for a very long time. It’s a very small community and this is when I got to know Paul Graham. We got into YCombinator and built this payment API for developers — many of our friends were doing startups so we had a strong sense on what the community wanted.
We just started showing it to people and getting feedback. All of our first 20–30 users came from YC companies or friends we knew. From there people talked to each other about it — payments were a big pain point.
John Lilly: One of my rules of thumb is put yourself around people who are doing interesting things — then when you do interesting things, other people can see and use it.
What is it like to start a company with your brother?
Patrick Collison: It was good because we were really good at resolving our differences, we had 20 years of fighting between us as experience.
It’s a common case with high growth startups where the co-founding team breaks up — generally it's hard to get the team to persist. It’s easy to stick with it when you have known the person for decades either as a friend or family.
It’s inevitable that tough situations will come up, but it’s how you react that is the challenge.
John Lilly: You also are known for reading a lot of history (including physical books), why do you think it's important to study history?
Patrick Collison: It’s a way to cheat. You could try to pound your head against the wall and think of original ideas — or you can cheat by reading them in books.
If you look at people like Douglas Engelbart— he didn’t just invent the mouse, it was so much more than that.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yJDv-zdhzMY
In his famous video “the mother of all demos” — he invented real time collaboration (Etherpad), video conferencing, live syncing of documents with video conferencing. The way I see it, he invented a better “Google Hangouts” system — and this was in 1968.
What’s surprising is so much of this early work is better than what we have today. Sure we have solved all kinds of scaling issues, deployments, technology — but the core problems of helping people work together were thought about more back then. Their concept of technology was the empowering of humans through technology.
So much of software today is either about 1) basic utility or 2) entertainment vs. the enabling of people. It’s much harder to see the latter. One phrase we use at Stripe is “most tech companies are building cars, Stripe is building roads.”
John Lilly: How big is Stripe now?
Patrick Collison: We are at 330 people and we are around 5 years old now depending on how you count when Stripe started — we launched on September 30, 2011 but had the idea in 2010.
John Lilly: And a year ago Stripe was half this size?
Patrick Collison: Yes a year ago we were 160 people, we're doubling every year.
John Lilly: Can you talk about how Stripe is organized? Do you have VP’s, managers, etc?
Patrick Collison: We are conventionally organized. As founders we have a tendency to do things differently. It comes from a good place but there are two problems with innovating on the organizational model:
1. The standard means of organization helped create Google/Facebook/Amazon/etc. Even if you could organize a company in a better way, do you need to do that?
2. Any alternative you do create, it's hard to get exposure and experience in that model.
Technology tends to change a lot but people and the organization of people are the same systems today.
However, there are areas for improvement. For example with interviewing, everyone copies how other companies interview but even Google has learned that there is no correlation between GPA and performance at the company. Algorithmic style coding interviews using whiteboards don’t work — and even then no one does anything different — this is crazy.
We don’t do this. When people interview at Stripe they write code using their own laptop in any language they like.
John Lilly: Stripe has been known for being excellent at hiring, how long did it take for you guys to move towards interviewing with personal laptops?
Patrick Collison: It’s always hard to know if things are good or bad, or what to attribute success to.
One of our biggest benefits is we were building software for engineers therefore we had a higher affinity towards engineers — how much did this matter? It’s hard to know.
The biggest thing for us was being ok with taking a really long time to hire great people. It took us 6 months to hire the first 2 people at Stripe. The next 6 months after that we hired 3–4 people. We did week long trials with many people and many people didn’t want to join after that.
In some cases we were having 3 month long conversations with people just to see if they would be interested in joining. When you are trying to hire great people there are two options:
1. Filter by expressed interest — secondary filter for who is good or not.
2. Find great people and then convert them to expressed interest.
We took the second approach but it was difficult. Multiple people at Stripe took several years to hire, 5 people took 3+ years to hire. The idea of taking 3+ years to hire someone is crazy, our biggest thing was being way more persistent than others and being ok with taking longer than any reasonable person would.
John Lilly: It sounds like you had product market fit right at the beginning? Was that true?
Patrick Collison: Yes and no.
Yes, in the very beginning — the idea of a credit card API and being easy to implement it in your own product — turned out to be a good idea.
No, from the fact that all of the additional products where we saw real product market fit — happened on top of the first layer we built. For example — Stripe connect — was a product we build for marketplaces (Instacart, Shyp, Postmates, Lyft, Uber) which helps them facilitate transactions between the people on their platforms. The vast majority of these marketplaces are not built on stripe — we had to produce all of these additional API’s on top of our original transaction layer we built.
John Lilly: It sounds like there were successive product market fits spread out over time — fast forward to 2015 — what scale are you at today? How do you talk about scale?
Patrick Collison: We don’t really talk about numbers but we help transact billions of dollars a year and have 100,000’s of businesses on the platform.
John Lilly: When you talk about Stripe Connect, isn’t this the same as PayPal? A peer to peer (P2P) payment network?
Patrick Collison: On the surface level it looks the same.
When you look one level deeper however the critical difference is what Lyft needs to facilitate transactions is very different than what you need to pay your friend.
Lyft is a business mediating the payment — what happens when a ride gets refunded, dealing with tips, chargebacks, different currencies, etc. It’s a very different problem set.
Question from the audience: How did you set up the relationships early with banks?
In the beginning we worked with a friend who ran a payments company in the midwest, we basically built our prototype on top of his platform. In fact in the early days we were processing many of the payments by hand manually.
This didn’t scale however so we asked one of our investors Geoff Ralston to help us with this problem. Jeff had previously started LaLa one of the first legitimate music services which set up relationships with all of the music labels.
He introduced us to Billy Alvarado who actually joined Stripe when we were a 5 person company. He was a business development person — not an engineer — and we were very torn over this decision. The team thought “he doesn’t write code, what is he going to do?”
After much anguish we hired him and he has become one of the most important people in the company. He solved all of our early banking relationship problems and helped teach us how startups could partner with traditional companies.John Lilly: Given Stripe had a clear value proposition from the early days — how did you decide what new things to do?
Patrick Collison: This is a tough question. Customers typically don’t know what they want and it’s difficult the find the answers through analysis.
Luckily when you are developing product developers, you can cheat and just ask them what you want — developers are outspoken and will also ask you directly. Next the challenge is how to prioritize various features. We spend a lot of time getting to know which customers have good judgements about our product.
There is a quote from Mark Zuckerberg which I like on this subject which is “What’s optional for a 1 year time horizon, might not be good for a 5 year time horizon.” It’s our job to have good judgement on how the pieces fit together.
I’d say about 70% of our new product ideas comes from listening to customers with good judgement and 30% are doing things which ought to be big but aren’t being asked for.
John Lilly: How’s Bitcoin related to all of this? Developers get excited about Bitcoin but it doesn’t feel like it’s a thing yet? Huge yet?
Patrick Collison: Bitcoin is definitely not huge yet.
However you can’t judge decisions by their direct outcomes — instead you should judge decisions by their expected value.
For Bitcoin it’s still small but the potential outcome could be significant. In addition we felt some general affinity towards the Bitcoin community. The distributed systems, decentralized infrastructure, and system of ledger are all cool concepts. Also the original Satoshi paper is one of the first papers which is both a CS breakthrough and a political philosophy breakthrough. So we decided to help out a bit.
John Lilly: Who runs product at Stripe?
Patrick Collison: We don’t have separate engineering and product teams within Stripe, everything is broken up by product engineering teams with one manager for each team. Then I manage all of the managers.
John Lilly: I’ve noticed when the CEO and founders are in the details of the product — this creates instability for the VP of product. Either the CEO is the micromanager of product, the CEO lets the VP of Product run with it however they want, or the CEO just doesn’t care. It’s a tough role to be in.
Patrick Collison: I agree it’s a tough role. Given we don’t have a separate product role, I am fairly involved in our product. The head of product helps staff, build, operate, and works with me.
John Lilly: Has there been any products Stripe has launched and killed?
Patrick Collison: Part of the thing that makes this difficult to answer is — product innovation tends to slow down as you scale.
We built the first product with 3 people, now we are at 300, why are we slowing down? Even as a user of Airbnb/Dropbox/etc., as a user I feel like the innovation has stopped.
What happens is so much additional investment is required to keep up with growth — the original 3 people can’t operate the full system anymore, it’s just so much bigger than when it started.
When you are looking to scale and innovate — this requires hiring many people, training lots of people, having them learn in this emergent process, figuring out how to coordinate together, etc. The net effect is product advances slower.
However, if you do a good job at this, you can look back and scale much faster once your systems are in place. Facebook did a good job at this: moved fast, went into a slow period, then moved quickly again.
At Stripe we haven’t launched many discrete new products — we have shipped many features and experiments but we haven’t killed any full new products. Because we have developers as customers — we run many ideas by them in idea/beta phases and if they don't like it, we stop. Before anything gets to launch we have had pretty exhaustive validation.
John Lilly: Moving to a management question, what do you do differently now that you are past 150 people (Dunbar’s number)?
Patrick Collison: Past 150 has been a big change for us. The biggest is a need for formal explicit communication — specifically broadcast community.
This kind of communication feels very unnatural — no one wakes up in the morning and sets out to talk in bullet points — Here is our 3 bullet points for our Q4 strategy. When you think about it, a startup itself is not a natural environment, there are different things you do that you would not do naturally. You don’t typically grow your social circle 50–100% year over year.
All of the discussions and debates we have — many of our current team weren’t there when we had those. 50% of our entire team wasn’t there a year ago. Each new person that comes in wants to do things different. Part of it is good in the fact that it re-opens issues and part of it is bad, given new people don’t have the full context.
John Lilly: One of the profound realizations I had is the CEO learns really fast but the various layers of the company can't possibly absorb all of this change — so I moved towards speaking consistently and getting alignment the bigger we got.
Patrick Collison: It’s delicate because we want alignment but at the same time if it becomes too rigid it will be problematic because people will want to quit. We need the right amount of structure.
One caveat to all of this is I don’t want to be the person to make all of the decisions — I don't want the organization to do everything I want to do — I want them to do the right things.
When I look at “The Stripe way” it’s a distinct thing and a distinct way of doing things. Sure I am close to it but it’s changing and evolving with all of the people we bring on.
One thing I have noticed for myself is a general shift from speaking to writing. It’s not that I need to speak less but I need to add more writing to my communication. Speaking can only happen once but writing can persist through time, be revised, be updated, and help in clarity.
One of my favorite insights which has had a big impact is a paper from Bruno Latour about the scientific revolution. His argument was the printing press was critical but it’s not because the printing press led to more distribution.
It was because written word was more concrete and rigid. Previously most scientific information traveled through spoken word — but when you are hearing about something from a friend, of a friend, of a friend — who knows how much the facts have shifted. It’s tough to know what is true vs. not.
In writing you can point to paragraph 3, line 2 and can disagree with that point specifically.
Question from the audience: How has the transition from developer to manager been for you?
Patrick Collison: It still pains me, I miss coding. I get this question a lot and yes I miss it a lot.
I think our industry over biases towards founders and the CEO — the media wants to associate a company with the person. The degree in which I am representing of Stripe is overrepresented compared to everyone one. It wasn’t just my transition to management — it was the whole team's transition to a large company.
(I didn’t write down who Patrick was quoting but) Basically the CEO’s job can be reduced to three things:
- Strategy
- Culture (No other person besides the founder/CEO can affect culture to the same degree)
- Selecting senior management of the company (hard for anyone else to do this job) — these people will be the domain experts of their function who are better than you.
- Optional is the product — the CEO can be the head of product or lead some specific function of the product.
That’s it. It’s easy if you can get these pieces right, it’s very difficult to fix things when they aren’t working. It’s a bit simplistic but it’s been helpful for me.

Stripe co-founder Patrick Collison on what most startups get wrong pre-product/market fit: “The main thing that I think companies screw up at the pre-product/market fit stage is speed of iteration. I was thinking, 80% of strategy is saying no. When I scaled my first startup, focus beat hustle. I was busy all day, but the needle didn't budge. Busy is easy; progress is hard. So I killed a flashy partnership pilot and three side projects. We won back two weeks, saved cash, and shipped what mattered. No one outside noticed the cuts. Inside, the team breathed, and our one number finally moved. - Define one metric that matters this quarter. - Cut initiatives that don't move that metric. - Schedule 'no' time: weekly review to stop or start. - Park ideas in a later list, not your calendar. - Say no to one meeting and one feature this week.

Great execution isn’t about speed — it’s about clarity under pressure. In software and startups alike, we often celebrate hustle. But real momentum comes from aligning every decision to a single, shared purpose. When priorities are clear, execution feels effortless — because everyone knows not just what to do, but why it matters. The teams that win aren’t the ones that move fastest. CEO Patrick Collison's email to Stripe employees | Mark Stouse | 57 comments
Agree & Join LinkedIn
# Mark Stouse’s Post
Mark Stouse
3y Edited
I'm sure that many people will have many different opinions on this announcement by Stripe's CEO. But as another CEO of another SaaS company, his message really resonated with me. And I want to shine a bright light on it. Collison wrote the truth as clearly as he could see it, and he owned his mistakes without equivocation. And he made sure that those people impacted by the Stripe layoff were not only taken care of financially but honored and appreciated. Is it perfect? Hell no! The whole situation sucks. But against that backdrop, Collison's decisions shine all the more brightly. We've seen so many CEOs chicken out, or flat do the wrong thing in similar circumstances. Collision had the same choice. He made the right one. He honored the people reading his note by pulling back the curtain on the massive complexity and pain that is involved in leading a company through the shit. He didn't do it as a smoke screen or as an excuse. There was no "poor pitiful me" sob story or self-serving act of professional seppuku. We've all seen so many leaders use their all-hands message to deflect the blame that we expect that sort of thing from all of them, right? I don't know Patrick or his team. Not a single person. But based on this note, I sure would like to. Something tells me that we are about to enter some Class 5 white water. I'm sure you feel it too. When we come out the other end, we probably will have lost a lot. Maybe even more than we were ever prepared to lose. We need to stand together to weather this storm. That means those who have power in our positions will have to be a lot more worthy of that power and position, or step down. But it also means that we have the chance to reform the bonds of love and concern that have become so frayed. It's our choice. Yours, mine, theirs, and Patrick's too. My name is Mark Stouse. I'm the founder and CEO of Proof Analytics. You may know of me, or not. I'm actually a big deal to two boys and a few friends and colleagues, but against the backdrop of the wider world, I'm just one guy. I've been on my own journey from hubris to humility for quite some years. Over the past five years, I think I've done a lot of good, and I've made a lot of mistakes too. Let me just say, if you want to find the hollow and brittle parts of yourself, the stupidity as well as the brilliance, start a SaaS business. I can't say whether it will make you a fortune, but it will definitely make you a better person. Or not. It's kind of binary that way. At Proof we are doing a lot of deep thinking about what we need to do next -- for ourselves, our customers, and those who might need help. Lord knows we've been helped a lot. We continue to be helped a lot. If you read this note and have something you'd like to share, please feel free to reach out to me. My email is mark.stouse@proofanalytics.ai. Thanks for reading Collison’s note to his employees. It’s a keeper.
CEO Patrick Collison's email to Stripe employees stripe.com
