Patrick Collison Explains How Stripe Changes Capitalism
Agree & Join LinkedIn
Getty Images for Wired 25
This weekend, at Wired's 25th Anniversary festival, I had the opportunity to speak with some brilliant people from the Wired world, including Stacy Brown-Philpot of TaskRabbit, Jeff Weiner of LinkedIn, Jack Dorsey of Twitter, and Jaron Lanier of Microsoft. All of the transcripts, lightly edited, will be posted here at LinkedIn. You can also see the full videos here.
NT: Stripe is a an extraordinary company. You have two products that most many people know. One is payments processing. If you're a startup, don't worry about banks and don't want worry about the law. You can just offload payments to Stripe and put in a couple of lines of code. And then there’s a product called Strike Atlas, and that solves a different problem, which is if you want to incorporate an entire company, they will do it for you! In fact, 20 percent of all new technology companies incorporated in Delaware are through Stripe Atlas.
Alright, so my first question. When Stripe started and you read about it, it was always about startups: often open source, idealistic startups. But then you also started doing payments for like Amazon, Facebook, Apple. And now you do payments for offline companies. So my question for you is, Is Stripe changing the way capitalism works or is Stripe just enabling the capitalism that we have now, but making it slightly more efficient in every way?
PC: Well it's a big question to start with.
NT: It’s the first question of the weekend!
PC: WIRED has been, since I was just a very small bit older than 5, sort of a symbol of technology optimism and hope for me. And I was reading Louis Rossetto's piece in the 25th anniversary issue on a trip last week. And I loved his articulation situating WIRED in the culture, and for the need now in this kind of environment of growing pessimism for a kind of militant optimism. So three cheers for that.
But to your question, I always like the economist Deirdre McCloskey’s rejection of the notion of capitalism, or rather, the fixation in the the word capitalism on capital. In her terms, capitalism represents a kind of mistaken nomenclature, and that actually the core of capitalism is opportunity. And so I think Stripe is a kind of digital, online version of that. What we're trying to do is to sort of build economic infrastructure for the Internet and to sort of try to create opportunities for people to build products, implant business models, expand globally to take something that they can build in their bedroom and then deploy it at full planetary scale. And it’s important to do so in such a way that the the most important tools and the most sophisticated advanced functionalities are available, not only to the biggest companies in the world, but also, I mean, to the two people starting out in their bedroom.
You mentioned some of the really large companies that use Stripe. But what I love about their use of Stripe is that in almost every case, they're using the same version of Stripe that, you know, an Atlas founder in Nigeria just starting out can take advantage of. And so, internally as we articulate Stripe’s long-term goals, one of—the third one in fact—is that we try to be an anti-incumbency force. We want to kind of reduce activation energy barriers. We want to make incumbency less of a competitive advantage. I mean we're still a small company in the scheme of things. But on net, we're arming the upstarts.
NT: The anti-incumbency bias is a super interesting point. And I understand that you do help the incumbents, but you help them with the same code that you help the Egyptian startups. But how far would you take this though? Stripe’s getting pretty big. You're taking an ever-larger portion of the payment space. When do you become a dominant incumbent? And when you reach that position, do you worry about the effect you'll have on the economy? We have a monopolist-centered tech economy. You're going to become one of those at your current trajectory. What will you do when you become a giant monopolist?
PC: There's a long way to go before top of our list of concerns is our excessive size.
NT: Thought experiment! What would you do? None of the people who've reached that point have given it up.
PC: OK, so three points. First, part of the reason we have kind of Stripe being an anti-incumbency force as being one of our explicit, written-down, long-term goals is so that we're kind of bound to it. And so we can't decide if and when we become sort of some much larger pillar of the economy, that actually, hey, you know, those upstarts aren't so great after all. Let's just sort of hope the cozy club of the entities which prevail around us in that moment.
Second is I think that, I'm not sure this narrative of tech monopoly is true. I don't want to say that it's completely untrue, but if you look at it just kind of a tech sort of market cap concentration, the tech industry does not appear to be more concentrated today than it was 10 years ago, 20 years ago, 30 years ago. If you just look at it over time, it's been sort of—it's been fairly consistent.
NT: Certain verticals get more concentrated, but there's so many more verticals, the total amount of concentration stays the same?
PC: Not even that. If you just look S&P 500 a percentage of the technology sector occupied by the top four technology companies, that does not seem to be more concentrated than it was in the past. However there is something, I think, important going on here, which is economy wide, we are definitely seeing much more concentration. And so, you know, there are kind of various studies around this, but you just take the median industry by either market cap or by revenue is getting more concentrated. And anecdotally we see this. Thirty years ago there are many airlines, now there’s only a very small handful. Thirty years ago there were many banks, now it’s a very small handful. I mean, you look at it globally—semiconductors, right. Thirty years ago, there were many different kinds of semiconductor companies. And now you know that there's really only three meaningful ones, not just in the U.S. but sort of on a global basis.
And so I almost wonder if we have the narrative backwards where, and again I don't want to be dismissive of some of the important questions that exist around technology companies, but to me it almost looks like the tech industry is not more monopolistic than it has been in the past. However, every other sector is certainly getting more concentrated. And then you have those nuanced questions around, well should we be looking at revenue concentration, or should we be looking at monopoly pricing? And obviously with regard to the tech giants in particular, that's kind of a subtle question because on the one hand, they're doing a lot, but on the other hand, they aren’t charging a lot.
NT: No, Facebook is very inexpensive to use, unless you put a specific value on your privacy.
PC: Right. So I'll pause there.
NT: OK. Let me go back and I want to ask you one of the questions about what got us to this point. You've spoken eloquently in previous interview, and you have said they were decisions made in the past 25 years about global Internet architecture which were good, and those were a lot of decisions that were made back in 1993, and the period shortly thereafter. And then there are a bunch of decisions made about global Internet architecture which were bad. And those led to some of the problems we have today. Explain some of the decisions that were bad and explain how we can make better decisions in the next quarter century.
PC: Keeping going with the softballs! So OK, I think one of the most amazing kind of intellectual exercises of the past 50 years is the overall architecture of the internet. And, you know, not just that which happened around the creation of the web and HTML and all the rest, but, you know, that which preceded it—TCP and the Ethernet and the core protocols and everything else. And to your point they were so carefully thought through and that the scale and variance and all the rest is really so amazing. Jeff Dean at Google talks about how sort of any technology you invent that sort of grows by more than towards the magnitude, you generally have to kind of to re-architect from scratch. And what’s so amazing about the Internet is, like, these guys invented in the late 60s 70s 80s and so on. And it's grown by sort of you know a dozen orders of magnitude, and we haven't had to rebuild the core technology to a large degree.
OK, what’s bad? So you know speaking our own narrow solipsistic book: payments are bad. Maybe that's good or bad from our standpoint; it certainly yielded an opportunity for us. But you know it is very funny to go back and look at the early error codes in the HTTP specification. There is this 404 we're all familiar with. And then there’s 402, sort of the payment required error code, and it sort of there in the spec as “to be implemented.” And that you know for such a kind of core piece of functionality of the Internet, it’s kind of funny or tragic that so many decades into the Web's history, and given the sort of central importance of being able to generate that sort of sustaining income from the internet, that's it has gone so undone and under-built.
There's lots of other aspects as well. Alan Kay talks about how remarkable a culture and singular the group was that invented the core protocols of the Internet. You know the folks at Park and MIT and this Cambrian explosion of innovation. And so I guess the question I find myself thinking about and wondering about is, well was there a particular ethos and culture and sort of set of connections and individuals and kind of sets of you know mentors and inspiration they had that really served us well in, say, the 25 years before 1993. Has some of that been lost now and to the extent that it’s been lost, how do we regain it?
NT: Let me dig into one subset of that. So you're talking about core payments early on—one of my favorite facts about Stripe is that the way they raised money is they went to the founders of PayPal and were like, “Payments are broken, give us money! And they got it, which is extraordinary. If payments had worked better in the early Internet, is there an alternative history whereby the most important companies that were to develop decided to make their revenue on selling things, whether subscriptions or something else, as opposed to advertising? Because that's like one of the foundational decisions that leads to the attention economy.
PC: Yes. It was funny trying to raise money from the PayPal people in that, I remember reflecting after my first meeting with Peter Thiel. I was cycling back from the Presidio and thinking, “Shit, you know, it probably wasn't such a good idea to tell them that PayPal is terrible.” But Peter is such a contrarian thinker, that he kind of told me years later that he didn't think PayPal was terrible, but he thought he was probably too biased to think that PayPal was good. And so the fact that I was making this other argument was kind of a reason he should invest in Stripe. And so I don't quite know how all the logic works, but, you know, we benefited from it so I won't complain. But alright!
So when we started Stripe, this was part of our thesis, that kind of on the margins, there was more advertising than there would be if payments worked well. Many things were kind of using ads because ads were easy, payments were hard. And you can shift the balance in the other direction. That was just kind of a hypothesis with a clear counterfactual back when we were starting Stripe. Now as the Chinese internet has burgeoned, it's actually become much more clear that this is indeed the case, at least in as much as you believe that sort of you know Chinese consumer preferences are representative of American consumer preferences. And so, for example, in the podcast space, you know, we're all familiar with the sort of the services that we know and love from listen to our favorite podcasts or hearing their endorsement every ten or fifteen minutes. But in China, the kind of prevailing monetization mechanism for podcasts is payments, just like it's totally different. I mean it’s the same product, but it’s monetized completely differently. And incidentally the Chinese podcast economy appears to be sort of quite a bit more lucrative—and there’s all this other stuff you have to normalize for, now what are they competing against and so on. But as a general matter, it seems to work very well there. So yes. In short I would say I agree.
NT: Right.
PC: But I'm biased.
NT: Alright. Last question since we're almost out of time. WIRED started in these offices because Silicon Valley was blowing up around these offices. We moved here in 1994. Stripe is right around the corner. You're enabling entrepreneurs all over the world. Is Silicon Valley still going to be like this in 25 years? Or will we have finally dispersed?
PC: I think that the returns agglomeration are very high right. And you know Paul Romer just won the Nobel Prize and he's obviously one of the foremost scholars of this. And so I think, absent self sabotage, Silicon Valley should—I don’t mean normatively should—remain substantially preeminent. It obviously has had kind of really remarkable durability since the 30s, or since Fairchild, or whatever.
However there’s this is kind of emergent self-sabotage that's occurring in the form of this kind of a trifecta: first of land prices, and housing costs and so on; second, of a sort of domestic mobility restriction. It's becoming ever harder for people who aren't benefiting from the opportunities in Silicon Valley to come here and to do so. And then there is an international mobility barrier. Immigration laws and rules have always been bad—or at least, they’ve never been great on the margins and maybe getting slightly worse. I also think the comparative advantage of the U.S. is diminishing.
And then thirdly, a kind of cultural change. Others can decide whether it's for good or ill. But you know I was just talking to Louis and he was just saying how today in Silicon Valley those who are positive, those who are optimistic, they are now the revolutionaries and the counterculture. And that's a remarkable statement. We're supposed to be the epicenter of innovation in the world. And it is now the case that there is such a kind of a prevailing mood and whether of cynicism and pessimism and negativity, that I think it does, in fact, substantially challenge our fundamental role and more importantly our fundamental opportunity. And I think it's so easy sitting here—San Francisco is rarely accused of being kind of a representative example of the sort of what it's like to exist you know in just some random place in the world. It is, you know, San Francisco is not normal. And I think in many ways that's good. But the downside is that, you know, since WIRED was founded, the size of the global middle class has tripled. And now more than half the world's population is in the middle class. Everyone here is familiar I'm sure with the sort of the endless statistics about how kind of things are getting better, and yet somehow kind that is not internalized in our mood. The mood is somehow that things are getting worse. And I think sort of a central question for the next 25 years is where does that mood go? Because you know ideology and symbolism and aspirations really matter.
NT: Alright. Well, my mood is better having listened to this man for 20 minutes. So thank you very much. We will see you at Wired 2043.

Stripe CEO Patrick Collison shares the tactics he used for finding product/market fit “We tried very hard to understand in granular detail what exactly it was that people were doing, where they were tripping up and so on.” Patrick gives some examples of specific tactics: • A public chat room to provide support to people integrating Stripe • For the first 10 users of Stripe, every API request sent an email to the founders so they could better understand how users were using their product and see if users were doing anything weird • All errors generated a high-priority email to the founders. This created a pleasant user experience where 15 minutes after hitting an error, Patrick could reach out to them and let them know the issue was fixed “These are all kind of examples of a general pattern of trying to be hyper-attentive to all the micro details of what people were doing in the product and iterating rapidly in response to it. Generally speaking, I think pre-product/market fit metrics are actually relatively unhelpful because probably not that many people are using your product. If it’s 20 users, you can in some sense afford to just look at everything they’re doing to understand what’s working and what isn’t.” Another example of this Patrick gives is embedding a text input on each of their web pages with placeholder text prompting users to give them useful feedback(e.g. “The worst thing about Stripe is…”, “The worst thing about this page is…”, or “I really hate the way Stripe does…”). As Patrick explains: “At that stage, you have to be kind of masochistic. We’d always be waking up to all these emails telling us all the terrible things about Stripe. But that was a helpful to-do list for the day ahead. Video source: Y Combinator(2018)

I’ve spent years in payments, and for a long time the conversation was pretty predictable. Auth rates. Fees. Uptime. All important. All table stakes. But the conversation is shifting… ⸻ 1/ What I’m seeing now — especially with AI-native and fast-growing products — is that those aren’t the questions founders are stuck on anymore. The real friction sits above the transaction. ⸻ 2/ At the same time, many traditional PSPs are still leading with: “Look how much we improved your authorization rate.” Useful, yes. But increasingly… not the problem builders are losing sleep over. ⸻ 3/ The harder questions I hear are about monetization: • How do we price something that’s usage-based without confusing customers? • How do we bill fairly when spend isn’t predictable? • How do we scale revenue without rebuilding billing logic every few months? ⸻ 4/ That’s why what Stripe is doing feels like a different bet. Less about processing payments. More about helping businesses design how money flows through the product from day one. ⸻ 5/ Take Lovable. What started as subscriptions evolved into entity setup, usage-based billing, and a clear default for how their users get paid. That’s not a checkout decision — that’s product architecture. ⸻ 6/ The same thinking shows up in Stripe’s acquisition of Metronome. Not about more volume. About owning the hardest, least glamorous part of modern businesses: pricing and billing when usage is variable. ⸻ 7/ From what I see working with growing companies, AI products don’t price like classic SaaS — and forcing them to usually breaks something later. Which is why monetization is now a product decision, not a finance one. ⸻ 8/ Processing still matters. Auth rates still matter. They’re just no longer the differentiator. ⸻

Two weeks ago at Stripe Tour in NYC, the signal was clear: programmable dollars are moving into bank-grade operations. Last week, J.P. Morgan projected stablecoin demand hitting $1.4T by 2027. Now we're seeing the regulatory infrastructure catch up. Product teams now have clearer constraints to build against. If stablecoins are going to scale to everyday money, the rails need to be predictable, auditable, and simple end-to-end. That means: - Settlement windows you can plan around - Redemption at face value with clear timelines - Provenance and policy controls that pass review without extra work - One path from initiation to completion that doesn't fragment when value crosses issuers, chains, or wallets Stablecoins won't become everyday money just because they're cheap, instant, and borderless. Patrick Collison on the importance of beauty and craftsmanship when building products
“If Stripe is a monstrously successful business, but what we make isn’t beautiful, and Stripe doesn’t embody a culture of incredibly exacting craftsmanship, I’ll be much less happy. I think the returns to both of those things in the world are really high. I think even beyond the pecuniary or financial returns, the world’s just uglier than it needs to be… One can do things well or poorly, and beauty is not a rivalrous good.”
Patrick believes a commitment to craftsmanship and beauty played an important role in Stripe’s success:
“My intuition is that more of Stripe’s success than one would think is downstream of the fact that people like beautiful things—and for kind of rational reasons because what does a beautiful thing tell you? Well it tells you the person who made it really cared… And so if you care about the infrastructure being holistically good, indexing on the superficial characteristics that you can actually observe is not an irrational thing to do.”

Patrick Collison on what he wishes he did differently when scaling Stripe “I think one of the most pernicious mental models you can have is that you are on some growth curve… I think a much better mental model to have is that you’re serving some market, and then there’s the percentage of the market that you’re serving. And whatever percentage you are not serving, you just haven’t built the go-to-market functions and organization that’s brought the product to those market segments.” In the early days of Stripe, they assumed they were on some growth curve, but Patrick believes they could’ve accelerated their growth by viewing growth as a function of their go-to-market apparatus: “What we did not do, but what I wish we did, is six months after launch, we should’ve mapped out the concentric circles of our market.” As he suggests, Stripe should’ve started with very early stage startups then mapped out the larger set of all technical startups - not necessarily very early stage. Then continue this process in successive increments until they got to all companies handling online payments. Each step along the way, figuring out the size of each market, the fraction Stripe was currently serving, what it would take to serve more, and so on. And then work backwards from there: “What would the organization look like that was serving the entire market? Let’s just start building that organization, because the growth curve is under my control. Of course, it’s not 100% under your control, but I think it’s much more under your control than people tend to think.” Video source: Y Combinator(2018)

Two Years. 50 Users. The Painful Secret Behind Stripe’s Explosive Growth. John Collison remembers it vividly. “We started Stripe in 2009 and launched in 2011. I told Patrick, ‘How hard can it be?’ The answer: two years of difficulty.” Two years later, Stripe had just 50 users. Not 50,000. Not 5,000. Fifty. For most founders, that would feel like failure. For the Collison brothers, it became their greatest competitive advantage. “When you spend two years getting 50 users, it doesn’t feel like progress,” John admits. “But even a good idea can feel slow-going.” Here’s the paradox of progress: slowness can be strategic. Because Stripe wasn’t scaling fast, they could obsess over every user: • Every bug report triggered an immediate personal response. • Customers could message the founders directly, 24/7. • Bay Area users were invited into the office to integrate Stripe side-by-side with the team. • Feedback on the dashboard got a reply in under 10 minutes. That obsessive care created what John calls a “surprising viral effect.” Each delighted user told another. Word of mouth became Stripe’s growth engine — one that still powers it today. “The thing that allowed us to take off later,” John reflects, “was the fact that we spent so much time on those first users. We built the product we truly wanted — and a culture of taking abnormally good care of our customers.” What looks like slow growth from the outside often hides the foundation of long-term scale. The lesson: Don’t confuse early stillness for stagnation. Sometimes, the quiet years are the ones that build enduring companies. Oluwasoye P. Most founders think “grow faster” means “do more.” It doesn’t. It means pause, think, and then do better. Stripe didn’t scale because they hired faster. They scaled because they got smarter about how they operated. Here’s what their COO, Claire Hughes Johnson, figured out (and what most founders miss): When you grow fast chaos sneaks in disguised as “momentum.” You hire. You ship. You sprint. Then one day, you wake up and realise: Your company’s making decisions you wouldn’t have made yourself. That’s when the real work starts. To grow intentionally, not accidentally, Stripe asked 6 hard questions every founder should steal: 1️⃣ Have we documented how we make decisions? ⇢ If it’s not written down, it doesn’t scale. Period. 2️⃣ Do we have a structure that actually supports our goals? ⇢ Flat orgs sound cool until no one knows who’s accountable. 3️⃣ Who’s been successful here and why? ⇢ Pattern match what works. Multiply it. 4️⃣ Do we have a 5-year plan that’s simple and alive? ⇢ If your people can’t recite it, it’s useless. 5️⃣ Do we know how our people feel? ⇢ Engagement drives performance. Disconnection kills it. 6️⃣ Are we decentralising decisions? ⇢ Because the companies that win are the ones that teach everyone how to think like founders. Growth isn’t about adding more people, products, or features. It’s about building systems of trust and clarity that let everyone move faster without you. You don’t scale by pushing harder. You scale by creating an organisation that doesn’t need you to push.

70% of start ups fail in the first 3 years. This is a ridiculously large amount, I’ve been looking at reasons why and here are my thoughts: 1) Start ups get bad advice When starting a company, founders need support and help. Great advice tends to cost a lot of money. Bad advice costs even more. My advice to founders: build a strong network of supports / advisors to support your journey. 2) Lack of Financal Literacy Understanding numbers is important, it helps founders grow their company. Stripe found in 2003 it costs roughly £4m to start a digital company. Now it costs £4,000. Techonology is meaning it’s now easier than ever to run a business. My advice to founder: Control costs and bring your MVP to market in the most efficient way. 3) Lack of Talent The first hires in a business can make or break a company. With 3-6 month notice periods been normal, it’s cruical that the right hires are bought in. My advice to founders: Bring in senior hires as soon as possible but also remember to Hire Slow, Fire Fast

Most candidates think Big Tech offers all look the same. This just isn't true at all. Google vs. startups is a completely different game, and your strategy has to change. Big Tech Negotiation: ↳ Predictable RSU schedules ↳ Fixed salary bands ↳ Limited base flexibility You’re negotiating inside guardrails. Startup Negotiation: ↳ Everything’s on the table ↳ Wide comp ranges ↳ Equity % swings outcomes massively You have more leverage points. Example: • Google L6 = $400K total comp (steady, cash-like RSUs) • Series B startup = $180K + 0.15% equity (uncertain, but with upside) The math is different, the leverage is different, and the rules are different. Founders lose money when they negotiate like it’s Google while talking to a startup. Smart candidates adjust their strategy to the structure of the offer. Big Tech has policies. Startups have flexibility. Know the difference. P.S. ♻️ Repost and save if you want the startup vs. Big Tech negotiation framework.

John Collison: We only had 50 users two years after founding Stripe “We started working on Stripe in the Fall of 2009, and we launched Stripe in September 2011,” John Collison reflects. “I remember right at the beginning when we were starting it I said to Patrick [Collison], ‘Yeah let’s do it. How hard can it be?’ Which gives you a sense of our mindset. And the answer was: two years of difficulty. We had not predicted that.” John remembers feeling dejected when Stripe only had 50 users two years later: “When you spend two years getting 50 users, it doesn’t feel like a whole lot of progress. It feels like things are going pretty slow.” But this is one of the challenges of startups, he argues: “If you’re working on a startup that’s a bad idea, it’s going to feel like slow-going. But if you’re working on a startup that’s a good idea, it may feel like slow-going too.” Yet slow growth has a silver lining: “I think the thing that allowed us to take off in the subsequent years was the fact that since we were spending so much time on each one of those users; since we were hyper-focused on building a great product; and since we weren’t dealing with problems of scale yet, that allowed us to build the product that we wanted. Part of the culture that set in really early on was taking abnormally good care of those early users.” The Stripe founders would get an email or phone call anytime a user ran into a bug. When they sent the customer an email moments later alerting them that the bug was now fixed, people’s minds were blown. They set up a Campfire room that any customer could join and use to message John and Patrick at any hour of the day or night. And if a user was based in the Bay Area, the founders would invite them to come by the office and help integrate Stripe for them. In the Stripe dashboard they would prompt their customers for feedback and feature requests. Then the Stripe founders would reply to that feedback within 10 minutes. “What this meant was that even though the user growth was happening quite slowly in the early days,” John explains, “it actually had a pretty surprising viral effect where people had a good experience, they told their friends about it, and we were able to spread entirely through word-of-mouth even to this day.” Video source: Stanford Technology Ventures Program (STVP)(2015)

My reaction when acquaintance & strangers tell me (not even ask) - "What you're building at NextDoor.Company, any big tech can easily put a map and do what you do." I just smile and go back to building. Because this is the most common fear led, risk averse statement people make when they see something new that doesn't fit the existing ecosystem. When Airbnb came out, people said "who will book a stranger's home across the country when hotels exist?" When Uber launched, people said "who will get into a random person's car when taxis are regulated and safe?" When Stripe started, people said "PayPal already exists, and banks have been doing payments forever." When Notion began, people said "we already have Google Docs, Evernote, and a thousand note taking apps." Here's what I've learned after 13 years of building products and doing 0 to 1: Big tech sure can copy anything. They have unlimited resources, the best engineers, and massive distribution. But they usually end up loosing a few key things while they were growing to become this behemoth. 1. Unwavering user centricity: They stop obsessing about core user problems and become enterprise serving orgs (exceptions exist and cases differs) 2. Speed: They introduce hierarchy and processes which is anti-speed. A simple feature takes months to deploy. 3. Agility: They're so big that they can't just enter any market. Their lane is defined. They can't look in smaller lanes. A company in Series B is looking to get Series C which means high growth, high ticket sizes etc. Big tech builds for scale and shareholders. Startups builds for people with real problems (at least for the first few months and years. And the lucky ones get to retain this DNA and keep doing it for decades) And here's the thing that people miss: by the time big tech notices your idea is worth copying, you've already built relationships, iterated 50 times based on real user feedback, and created something that actually solves the problem in a way only you could. The "big tech can copy you" argument is just another way of saying "don't try." It's the same energy as "don't start a company because 90% fail" or "don't leave your job because it's risky." Yes, it's risky. Yes, someone with more resources could theoretically build what you're building. But they won't build it with your care, your understanding, or your commitment to the specific people you're serving. So, if your idea is good enough that folks say "big tech could copy it," congratulations. You're onto something real. Now go build it before you talk yourself out of it. 📸: From a recent panel discussion at uxindiaconf (ux-india.org)

Startups love to preach “move fast,” but half the stress in early-stage life comes from living three quarters into the future. You’re pitching a Series A in your head while debugging the MVP… worrying about next year’s hiring plan before you’ve even hired the first engineer… or refreshing your Stripe dashboard like it’s a glucose monitor. Here’s the truth: Momentum is built in the present. The next milestone only happens if you nail today’s tiny, unglamorous tasks. And staying in the moment actually helps your health and your company: You sleep better (less imaginary investor drama). You think clearer (no mental tabs open for “what-ifs”). Your team sees calm, not cortisol. So instead of forecasting Q2 2026, try this: Ship something small today. Call the candidate who ghosted you. Drink water that’s not in a coffee cup. Because the only thing worse than burning out… is burning calories worrying about things that haven’t even happened yet.

"I'm also talking to Google"; the startup recruiter's face falls. You just killed your startup negotiation. Here's why: Negotiation is like dating. Don't bring Henry Cavill to your date with a normal person. When you tell a Series B startup " Google offered me $350k," they think: → "Why would you ever choose us?" → "They'll leave the moment Google calls back" → "We can't compete, so why try?" Game over. Here's the rule that actually works: → Match your leverage to your audience. → Startup? Reference other startups. → FAANG? Reference other FAANGs. → Mid-stage? Reference other mid-stage companies. Real example: Robert was negotiating with a Barcelona startup. Instead of mentioning his Google offer, he said: "I'm excited about this role. I'm also in final rounds with [Similar Series B] and [Another European Startup]. Based on those conversations, I'd need to see a package around $X to make this move." Result? → 15% higher base salary → 0.25% more equity → Better contract protections The startup felt they could win. So they fought for him. The psychology: ✅ Credible - they believe you'd actually join ✅ Competitive - but within their league ✅ Respectful - you're not trying to intimidate Wrong leverage = dead negotiation Right leverage = bidding war Next time you negotiate, ask yourself: "Am I bringing Superman to a normal person date?" Have you ever accidentally used the wrong leverage and watched an offer disappear? P.S. I'm hosting a free live workshop on Wednesday at 3:00 PM PST all about crafting a successful counteroffer. Details in the comments!

Is Stripe better off at the moment as a private or a public company? Up to this point we have determined private. That could change at some point. People generally make the argument that public companies run in a more disciplined fashion. I think that's hogwash. Like if you need a 25 year old Fidelity analyst asking you to double click on your CapEx, blah, blah, blah to run the company with discipline, something is horribly wrong at the company and you need new management. There's always a tendency to be kind of pro cyclical and I think you need to be kind of particularly careful as a public financial services company to avoid some of those temptations and some of those tendencies. We're kind of a company at the intersection of financial services and technology. Being private for a long time is, is the norm. What matters is less kind of the returns in a given year and more duration. And so the question is, you know what enables the best compounding on sort of a 10 year time horizon and you know what's best for shareholders as you really take kind of the longer term perspective. And then just for customers at this juncture with the business growing at this rate, we want to spend the marginal hour with some customer. This is our life's work. You know we're not going anywhere. We'll be very happily running Stripe in 10 years time, in 20 years time.

In 2012, Patrick Collison, co-founder of Irish payments technology company Stripe, said it would have been too difficult to set up the company in Ireland, because of the banking and investment systems and the talent pool here. More than a decade on, LinkedIn News Europe asked his brother, fellow Stripe co-founder, John Collison, if this would still be the case. For fintechs in particular, John Collison says, the regulatory environment has become easier, and startups in the space in both Ireland and the UK have been embraced, such as Revolut or Monzo. And while the talent pool is bigger than it used to be, it can be challenging for startups to keep hold of people. Apple, Meta, Google and many other US tech titans have set up their European headquarters in Ireland. Asked if the large number of tech giants help or hinder Ireland's startup scene, John Collison said they have a positive impact overall. "You're seeing a great training-up effect of people where they've experienced working in tech," he says . "There's a larger pool to hire from. We see this at Stripe where we hire people in our Dublin office and then they go off and work at a startup there. We would love it if they stayed working at Stripe but we're also very happy for them that they end up working at a startup and contributing to the Irish internet economy that way." See John Collison's full answer on Ireland's regulatory and funding environment for startups below, and read the rest of the interview here: https://lnkd.in/gSBgCyRQ✍: Polly Dennison and Sam Shead📹: Solange Uwimana Source: https://lnkd.in/gMp44Qi5

The investment has gotten way better and honestly, I think it's just mostly not a factor these days where if you have a good startup idea, you're going to be able to raise money first. Like there's lots of things that make, you know, starting a startup hard, but firstly, you actually have more options to bootstrap like, you know, cloud service providers, you know, Stripe, things like that have made us, I think more feasible to bootstrap a company. But if you want to go raise investment, you can in this good investors in Ireland, you know, people like frontline, there's. Tons of great investors in the UK a very short distance away. And again, if you wanna raise money, you can go to the US and raise money there. So again, I think a lot of options for people on the regulatory environment. I think that's actually gotten quite a bit better for at least fintechs in particular since we started Stripe, where there's just been a movement over the last, you know, 12 years since then to create the right structures for fintech that are building. So we've had PSD two, we've had open banking, we've had, you know, some regulatory sandboxes, you know, in the UK. Here we've seen all these new banks pop up and they're now wildly popular, Monzo and Revolut and people like that. And so I would say there, I think the regulatory environment is actually gotten better because there's been an embrace of startups that you haven't gotten in every location around the world.

Two Years. 50 Users. The Painful Secret Behind Stripe’s Explosive Growth. John Collison remembers it vividly. “We started Stripe in 2009 and launched in 2011. I told Patrick, ‘How hard can it be?’ The answer: two years of difficulty.” Two years later, Stripe had just 50 users. Not 50,000. Not 5,000. Fifty. For most founders, that would feel like failure. For the Collison brothers, it became their greatest competitive advantage. “When you spend two years getting 50 users, it doesn’t feel like progress,” John admits. “But even a good idea can feel slow-going.” Here’s the paradox of progress: slowness can be strategic. Because Stripe wasn’t scaling fast, they could obsess over every user: • Every bug report triggered an immediate personal response. • Customers could message the founders directly, 24/7. • Bay Area users were invited into the office to integrate Stripe side-by-side with the team. • Feedback on the dashboard got a reply in under 10 minutes. That obsessive care created what John calls a “surprising viral effect.” Each delighted user told another. Word of mouth became Stripe’s growth engine — one that still powers it today. “The thing that allowed us to take off later,” John reflects, “was the fact that we spent so much time on those first users. We built the product we truly wanted — and a culture of taking abnormally good care of our customers.” What looks like slow growth from the outside often hides the foundation of long-term scale. The lesson: Don’t confuse early stillness for stagnation. Sometimes, the quiet years are the ones that build enduring companies. Oluwasoye P. A founder just shared they hit $5M ARR. After 10 years. No profit most years. Millions in debt to fix technical problems. The comments called it "failure." I call it reality. Everyone sees the unicorns. Nobody talks about the workhorses. The companies that take a decade to find product-market fit. That rebuild their tech stack three times. That almost die every other year. This founder fired dozens of people. Watched co-founders leave. Borrowed millions to fix technical debt. And kept going. Not because it was logical. Because they were "unreasonably optimistic." What resonated with me: They check Stripe 10 times a day, anxious about revenue. They built features nobody wanted for years. They chose persistence over intelligence. After 10 years, they're finally profitable. Their team has phantom equity worth millions. They might exit for $60M. That's not failure. That's most successful startups. The real insight: "It doesn't get easier, but you get better at handling it." I haven't built a $5M company (yet), but as someone building products and transitioning toward founding designer/design founder mode, this story matters. It's the antidote to unicorn mythology. We need more stories like this. The decade-long grinds. The technical debt nightmares. The co-founder divorces. The anxious Stripe refreshing. Because this is what building something real actually looks like.#StartupReality#Entrepreneurship#GrowthMindset#RealTalk#ProductDevelopment

70% of start ups fail in the first 3 years. This is a ridiculously large amount, I’ve been looking at reasons why and here are my thoughts: 1) Start ups get bad advice When starting a company, founders need support and help. Great advice tends to cost a lot of money. Bad advice costs even more. My advice to founders: build a strong network of supports / advisors to support your journey. 2) Lack of Financal Literacy Understanding numbers is important, it helps founders grow their company. Stripe found in 2003 it costs roughly £4m to start a digital company. Now it costs £4,000. Techonology is meaning it’s now easier than ever to run a business. My advice to founder: Control costs and bring your MVP to market in the most efficient way. 3) Lack of Talent The first hires in a business can make or break a company. With 3-6 month notice periods been normal, it’s cruical that the right hires are bought in. My advice to founders: Bring in senior hires as soon as possible but also remember to Hire Slow, Fire Fast

Pieter Theil ,co founder of PayPal had a crazy idea : Launch 12 startups in 12 months. Here’s what happened : Month 1: PlayMyInbox → Failed Month 2: GifBook → Failed Month 3: Tubelytics → Failed Month 6: NomadList → $30k/month Month 9: RemoteOK → $50k/month Most people would have quit after the first 3. Pieter didn’t. Why ? He wasn’t just trying to build better products. He was building better systems. By Month 12, his process looked like this: Launch time → 30 days → 3 days Customer feedback time → 2 weeks → 2 hours Revenue per hour → $0 → $125 The lesson: Don’t chase the perfect product. Or the perfect business. Work on the system that produces the perfect business or product. Because in the long run, systems compound. And the systems will take you to success. What’s one system you’re working on right now?#startingabusiness#systems#startersky#perfectbusiness#systemscompound

Stripe's most controversial acquisition created a $100M+ growth engine nobody saw coming In 2017, Stripe acquired Indie Hackers for an undisclosed sum, and the internet lost its mind. Why would a VC-backed payment giant buy a platform celebrating bootstrapped independence? It seemed like a contradiction. It wasn't. The backstory: • Indie Hackers was founded in 2016 by Courtland Allen to showcase profitable, self-funded businesses • Just 8 months after launch, it was generating $8K/month • Patrick Collison (Stripe CEO) personally reached out after discovering it on Hacker News The controversy was immediate: "This is peak hypocrisy! A VC-backed company buying the symbol of bootstrapped independence?" But Stripe's strategy was brilliant: 1. Keep Indie Hackers completely independent (no Stripe branding) 2. Provide resources for rapid community growth 3. Play the long game – supporting entrepreneurs creates future Stripe customers The results after 6 years: • 85,000+ newsletter subscribers (10X growth) • Thousands of profitable businesses launched • Most using Stripe as their payment processor • Ecosystem play generated $100M+ in processing volume • In 2023, the founders bought it back (with Stripe remaining an investor) The contrarian lesson: Sometimes the best acquisition strategy isn't about absorbing a competitor or technology – it's about nurturing an ecosystem that naturally feeds your core business. Stripe understood that every successful indie founder would eventually need payment processing. By helping more founders succeed, they created their own customer pipeline. Question: What other companies have made counterintuitive acquisitions that paid off in unexpected ways?

The best founders I know aren't trying to be 10x better. Every founder pitching me obsesses over the same thing: "We're 10x better than the competition." 10x faster. 10x cheaper. 10x more features. And they're solving for the wrong problem entirely. Here's the uncomfortable truth: Being 10x better at what everyone else is doing means you're still playing their game. The real 10x? It's not about doing the same thing better. It's about doing something different entirely. Think about it: Uber didn't build a better taxi company. They eliminated the need to own a taxi. Airbnb didn't build better hotels. They turned everyone's spare room into inventory. Stripe didn't build a better payment processor. They made payments invisible to developers. These companies weren't competing on features. They changed the entire game. The founders I'm betting on now? They're not showing me comparison charts against competitors. They're showing me why the comparison doesn't even make sense. Because when you're truly 10x, you're not better than the competition. You've made the competition irrelevant. Stop optimizing for the wrong game. Start creating your own.

These companies weren't competing on features. They changed the entire game. The founders I'm betting on now? They're not showing me comparison charts against competitors. They're showing me why the comparison doesn't even make sense. Because when you're truly 10x, you're not better than the competition. You've made the competition irrelevant. Stop optimizing for the wrong game. Start creating your own.

Stripe is reportedly in talks to buy back stock from venture investors at a $106.7B valuation. It’s a striking turn if you’ve been following their journey. A few years ago, critics mocked Stripe for not going public at $95B. Then the market swung, their paper valuation was marked down to $50B, and the narrative became that they “missed their window.” Today they’re back above $100B, not by ringing a bell on Wall Street, but by running a private process on their own terms. The public markets are no longer the sole scoreboard for company quality. The Collison brothers aren’t playing the old game of “IPO as arrival.” They’re playing a longer game, one where control, optionality, and timing matter more than headlines. Buybacks at this scale signal three things: 1/ Confidence - Stripe is consolidating ownership and strengthening its cap table. 2/ Maturity - A $100B+ private company can manage capital allocation the way a public one would, without the quarterly spotlight. 3/ Shifted norms - The playbook is changing. Great companies no longer prove themselves with a ticker symbol. They prove themselves by executing, compounding, and choosing when (or if) they want public scrutiny. For founders, the lesson is bigger than Stripe. The question is no longer “when do we IPO?” but “how do we keep control while still recycling capital and rewarding all stakeholders?” Stripe has been criticized for waiting. But how many companies could take a $50B markdown and come back stronger?

I've been consistently unimpressed (but feel the opposite about the announcement below for all those who don't make it past the ...), but sadly unsurprised, by how many companies have messaged and dealt with layoffs in the SaaS space over the past few months, especially from companies who shallowly proclaim "employees are family members". Layoffs are hard, and there's probably no perfect way to do it, but there are definitely wrong ways of doing it. One of the most frustrating elements of these layoffs is the lack of transparency, clarity, maturity, and empathy (forgetting and no longer being able to connect to and feel what it is to be an employee not in high-ranking leadership) exhibited in these announcements - usually under the guise of increased costs, the pandemic, or oncoming recession - all while crying on screen or proclaiming that it was unpreventable, inevitable, and unforeseen. The bottomline is that leadership at a company is primarily responsible to shareholders and investors, not employees. And when things get tight, pressure sets in, or mistakes must be corrected and reversed, workforce reductions will almost always happen before financial losses are stomached by investors, shareholders, and leadership. This should not be a surprise to anyone, and is part of the reality on which venture capital, and ultimately capitalism, is built. While that can be frustrating at times, it's the way things are. What's most frustrating is when a company talks to its employees like a group of children, is vague or untruthful about why reductions are happening, when they are happening, how they are happening, and what the future looks like moving forward, and instead, are told that a large amount of employees are being let go due to "increased server costs" (I did, indeed, witness that). This is often followed up by a desire to "give back" by creating a spreadsheet with all the employees' names they just couldn't bear losing and posting it on LinkedIn. I have been historically impressed by Patrick Collison and John Collison at Stripe, and the way they dealt with laying off 14% of their company today is no exception. Their announcement is worth reading carefully. https://lnkd.in/gTADem9t#layoffs#layoffsandjobreductions#saas#hiring

# Full transcript: Stripe CEO Patrick Collison on Recode Decode
“Startups are hard.”
Updated Jun 13, 2017, 6:28 PM UTC
Manuel Blondeau / Getty
On this episode of Recode Decode, hosted by Kara Swisher, Stripe CEO Patrick Collison dropped in to talk about startups(they’re hard to start, hard to make profitable), why he chose to do a back-end payments infrastructure company and how Silicon Valley, for all its faults, is still a place where good ideas are valued and risk of failure is accepted as the price of admission.
You can read some of the highlights from the interview at that link, or listen to it in the audio player below. We’ve also provided a lightly edited complete transcript of their conversation.

COLLISON: …You’ve written a lot about how the study of economics has influenced your appreciation for the arts, and for literature, and for food, and all of the rest. COWEN: This is a version of that. Here would be a simple example: If you think about Renaissance Florence, at its peak, its population, arguably, was between 60,000 and 80,000 people. And there were surrounding areas; you could debate the number. But they had some really quite remarkable achievements that have stood the test of time and lasted, and today have very high market value. Now, in very naive theories of economics, that shouldn’t be possible. People in Renaissance Florence, they didn’t produce a refrigerator that we’re still using or a tech company that we still consult.
But there’s something different about, say, the visual arts, where that was possible, and it was done with small numbers. So there’s something about the inputs to some kinds of production we don’t understand. I would suggest if we’re trying to figure out, like what makes Silicon Valley work, actually, by studying how they did what they did in the Florentine Renaissance is highly important. You learn what are the missing inputs that make for other kinds of miracles.
Ireland and writing would be another example.

# Stripe CEO Patrick Collison on management, rationalism, and the enlightenment
The Ezra Klein Show - see all episodes
Patrick Collison is the 28-year-old CEO of Stripe, the online payments company that was just valued at $9 billion.Haven't heard of Stripe? You've probably used it. Last year, 40 percent of people who bought something online used Stripe's payment systems. The company has become an integral part of the internet's financial plumbing. And Collison has become one of Silicon Valley's leading lights — he made the cover of Forbes last year, where one venture capitalist described him as "the LeBron James of entrepreneurs."Collison is also one of the few people I've met who is a genuine polymath. He seems to know everything about everything, and his recall — particularly his ability to live-footnote his own comments — is something to behold. We talk about how he and his brother conceived of, and launched, Stripe, and then we go much deeper. Among the topics we discussed: -Why there was a market opportunity for Stripe in a world that had PayPal-Why people are often wrong when they look at a market and think an incumbent has dominated it-What he thinks is untrue about the stereotypes of how Silicon Valley handles regulation-How we might be able to tell whether a buildup of regulations are preventing new companies from emerging-Why jobs like home healthcare and childcare are becoming tension points in our national immigration discussion-The difference in the way politicians and tech leaders approach problem-solving-How he tries to shape culture within his company to help it become, in his words, more like itself-What he admires about CEOs like Jeff Bezos and Jim Simons-The culture of "rationalist” bloggers, and why he reads them-How we underestimate the importance of the Enlightenment periodEnjoy!Learn more about your ad choices. # [The Knowledge Project Ep. #32] Patrick Collison: Earning Your Stripes
Shane: Patrick, I’m so happy to get to talk to you.
Patrick: Thanks very much for having me.

Interview: Patrick Collison, co-founder and CEO of Stripe
# Interview: Patrick Collison, co-founder and CEO of Stripe
But in addition to being an ace businessperson, Patrick is one of the most intellectually curious human beings I’ve ever met. Hanging out with Patrick will typically always involve him asking you to explain ideas in your area of specialization, which he will typically grasp with unusual alacrity; at least once, he threw a party that consisted of people giving him seminar talks on topics of their choice! His biggest intellectual interest is the idea of technological progress — what it means, why it happens, and how to encourage more of it. Among his ideas is the field Progress Studies, an interdisciplinary field that studies the history and determinants of technological advancement and how it feeds into social progress more generally.
In the wide-ranging email interview that follows, I turn the tables and ask Patrick to explain his own ideas to me! We discuss the future of technology, what kinds of innovation humanity needs, whether innovation has slowed down and what to do about it, the proper roles of government and the private sector, how research funding can be reformed, how international competition (including U.S.-China competition) figures into the equation, and much more!
N.S.: So, what are the three things that excite you most about the 2020s?
It's hard to restrict to three! But here are the first that jump to mind:
First, the explosive expansion in access to opportunity facilitated by the internet. Sounds prosaic but I think still underestimated. Several billion people recently immigrated to the world's most vibrant city and the system hasn't yet equilibrated. When you think about how YouTube is accelerating the dissemination of tacit knowledge, or the number of creative outsiders who can now deploy their talents productively, or the number of brilliant 18 year-olds who can now start companies from their bedrooms, or all the instances of improbable scenius that are springing up... in the landscape of the global commons, the internet is nitrogen fertilizer, and we still have a long way to go -- economically, culturally, scientifically, technologically, socially, and everything in between. I challenge anyone to watch this video and not feel optimistic.
Second, progress in biology. I think the 2020s are when we'll finally start to understand what's going on with RNA and neurons. Basically, the prevailing idea has been that connections between neurons are how cognition works. (And that’s what neural networks and deep learning are modeled after.) But it looks increasingly likely that stuff that happens inside the neurons -- and inside the connections -- is an important part of the story. One suggestion is that RNA is actually part of how neurons think and not just an incidental intermediate thing between the genome and proteins. Elsewhere, we're starting to spend more time investigating how the microbiome and the immune system...

#### The Stripe CEO flips the script for a special Conversations with Tyler.
A few months ago, Tyler asked Patrick Collison, CEO of Stripe, to be on the show. Patrick agreed, but only under the condition that he would do the interviewing. Thus, what follows is the conversation Patrick wanted to have with Tyler, not the one you wanted to have.
Happily Patrick stayed true to the spirit of Conversations with Tyler, and their dialogue covers a wide range of topics including the benefits of diverse monocultures, the state of macroeconomics, Donald Trump, the amazing and eclectic economics faculty at George Mason University, Peter Thiel, Brian Eno, Thomas Schelling, why Twitter is underrated, and — most pressing of all — why Marginal Revolution is so strange looking.

Patrick Collison on Innovation and Scientific Progress - Econlib
##### Patrick Collison on Innovation and Scientific Progress
Patrick Collison, co-founder and CEO of Stripe, talks with EconTalk host Russ Roberts about the pace of innovation. Collison argues that despite enormous increases in the numbers of scientists and researchers, the pace of progress in scientific and technological understanding does not seem to be increasing accordingly. The conversation looks at the challenge of measuring innovation and whether the pace of innovation should be a matter of concern and if so, what might be done about it.
Explore audio transcript, further reading that will help you delve deeper into this week’s episode, and vigorous conversations in the form of our comments section below.

2. The impatient pursuit of progress
# The impatient pursuit of progress
Patrick Collison returns to MIT to speak to students about the challenges and possibilities of entrepreneurship.
Lori LoTurco | Zain Humayun | School of Engineering
Email: loturco@mit.edu
School of Engineering
On Nov. 14, the School of Engineering and the Sloan School of Management co-organized an event for students with Patrick Collison (right), the CEO of internet payment company Stripe.
Photo: Lillie Paquette/School of Engineering
With the Cambridge, Massachusetts, skyline visible behind him and before a room full of students, Patrick Collison asked for a show of hands. “Who’s here because you’re interested in starting a company at some point?” About half the room raised their hands. “And who’s interested in going and working at a technology company?” Some more hands went up. “And [those] just here for the free food?” The students laughed.
On Nov. 14, the School of Engineering and the Sloan School of Management co-organized an event for students with Patrick Collison, CEO of internet payment company Stripe. Anantha Chandrakasan, dean of the School of Engineering and the Vannevar Bush Professor of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science, opened the event, appropriately entitled “The Impatient Pursuit of Progress,” by first introducing Collison, followed by the discussion’s moderator, Eric Grimson, MIT Chancellor for Academic Advancement and Bernard M. Gordon Professor of Medical Engineering. At the age of 16, Collison started at MIT to study math. Six months later, he left to start his first company, Auctomatic, with his brother John, who was attending Harvard University at that time. Auctomatic was acquired by LiveMedia in March 2008, after which Collison returned to MIT to pursue degrees in math and physics. After a year, he took a second leave of absence and co-founded Stripe with his brother John.
Today, organizations of all sizes, from startups to public companies like Salesforce and Amazon, use Stripe's software to accept online payments. More than 80 percent of people across the country made a purchase using Stripe in the past year. As of September, Stripe was valued at $35 billion. In 2016, Collison and his brother John became the world’s youngest self-made billionaires.

Patrick’s journey into programming started with a fascination with obscure programming languages. At 16, he created a programming language called Chroma, a Lisp dialect designed for web applications. Not long after, he built his first company—Auctomatic—entirely on Smalltalk. And while the architecture was elegant and the tool was powerful, there was one problem.
[...]
“Nobody ever benefited from it because Auctomatic had no users,” Patrick said. It was, he joked, “the world’s most elegantly designed userless company.”
[...]
That experience shaped one of Stripe’s founding philosophies: prioritize pragmatism over elegance.
[...]
“Every time there’s a super elegant way to do things and a practical, pragmatic way to do things, we’re just gonna cut the corner—at least until we validate that there’s actual user value here.”

- Stripe's CEO says he uses AI tools like xAI's Grok to answer "factual or empirical questions."
- But when it comes to writing, the fintech CEO draws the line.
- "I usually end up dissatisfied with the writing that they produce," said Patrick Collison.

Stripe's CEO, Patrick Collison, loves using AI to ask factual questions — just don't ask him to let it write for him.

In a conversation with Cursor's CEO, Michael Truell, which was uploaded to Cursor's YouTube channel on Tuesday, Collison said he relies on AI tools like xAI's Grok to answer questions while reading.

The CEO of the payments platform said he uses the tools "mainly for answering factual or empirical questions."

"I find them terrific for that," he added.

Collison said he uses Grok in voice mode, letting it run in the background while he reads so he can ask questions out loud. "The answers are very helpful," he added.

### Discover Level Up

Take smart steps toward the goals you've been thinking about. Try it now

But when it comes to writing, Collison draws the line.

"I wish they were useful for writing," he said. "But I usually end up dissatisfied with the writing that they produce."

What bothers him most is the lack of personal flair, even when he tries prompting the AI not to sound generic.

"My personal style differs from the personal style, so to speak, of the models," he said. "In some self-centered way, when I write, I want to use my personal style."

His comments reflect a view held by some tech leaders: AI may be great for research and brainstorming, but when it comes to voice and tone, human creativity still wins.

LinkedIn's chief operations officer, Dan Shapero, told Business Insider in April that while he turns to AI chatbots for tips on writing and presenting, he doesn't use LinkedIn's AI summary feature to write his own profile — he has lots of practice writing in first person.

Collision and Stripe did not respond to a request for comment from Business Insider.

## How CEOs are using AI

Tech CEOs have been vocal about how they weave AI into their daily lives.

Microsoft's CEO, Satya Nadella, said he uses Copilot to summarize his Outlook and Teams messages. He uses at least 10 custom agents from Copilot Studio to help with meeting prep and research, he said during a Bloomberg interview published in May.

He also uploads podcast transcripts to the Copilot app on his phone so he can discuss the content with a voice assistant during his commute.

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said he used AI "constantly" after welcoming his first child in February.

"Clearly, people have been able to take care of babies without ChatGPT for a long time," Altman said in an OpenAI podcast interview published last month. "I don't know how I would have done that."

Altman said he now mostly uses ChatGPT to research babies' developmental stages.

Nvidia's CEO, Jensen Huang, takes a more educational approach. He said he uses AI programs to learn new concepts.

"I use it as a tutor every day," Huang said during the Milken Institute Global Conference in May. "In areas that are fairly new to me, I might say, 'Start by explaining it to me like I'm a 12-year-old,' and then work your way up into a doctorate-level over time."

# Interview: Patrick Collison, co-founder and CEO of Stripe
[...]
In addition to being a friend and a Noahpinion subscriber, Patrick Collison is one of the world’s most successful founder-CEOs. Along with his co-founder and brother John, he built online payments company Stripe into a $36 billion behemoth in a decade. (Patrick and John hail from Ireland, continuing the hallowed tradition of Irish immigrants making it big in America.)
[...]
But in addition to being an ace businessperson, Patrick is one of the most intellectually curious human beings I’ve ever met. Hanging out with Patrick will typically always involve him asking you to explain ideas in your area of specialization, which he will typically grasp with unusual alacrity; at least once, he threw a party that consisted of people giving him seminar talks on topics of their choice! His biggest intellectual interest is the idea of technological progress — what it means, why it happens, and how to encourage more of it. Among his ideas is the field Progress Studies, an interdisciplinary field that studies the history and determinants of technological advancement and how it feeds into social progress more generally.
[...]

## Description
This episode first aired in May 2018. This is a Knowledge Project Podcast episode from the archive.
--
Patrick Collison, CEO, and co-founder of Stripe shares wise insights on success, failure, management, decision making, learning and so much more.
In this conversation, we cover the biggest (and most valuable) mistakes Patrick made in the early days of Stripe and how they helped him get better, the characteristics that Patrick looks for in a new hire to fit and contribute to the Stripe company culture, what compelled he and his brother to move forward with the early concept of Stripe, even though on paper it was doomed to fail from the start, the gaps Patrick saw in the market that dozens of other processing companies were missing — and how he capitalized on them, the lessons Patrick learned from scaling Stripe from two employees (he and his brother) to nearly 1,000 today, and so much more.

## Description
Patrick Collison (@patrickc) is chief executive officer and co-founder of Stripe, a technology company that builds economic infrastructure for the internet.
After experiencing firsthand how difficult it was to set up an online business, Patrick and his brother John started Stripe in 2010. Their goal was to make accepting payments on the internet simpler and more inclusive. Today, Stripe powers millions of online businesses around the world.
Prior to Stripe, Patrick co-founded Auctomatic, which was acquired by Live Current Media for $5 million in March 2008. In 2016, he was named a Presidential Ambassador for Global Entrepreneurship by President Obama. Originally from Limerick, Ireland, Patrick now lives in San Francisco where Stripe is headquartered.
Also, as you can tell from seeing just a selected segment of his reading list shared in the show notes below, he’s one of the most well-read people I know. Please enjoy!

## Description
A few months ago, Tyler asked Patrick Collison, CEO of Stripe, to be on the show. Patrick agreed, but only under the condition that the be the one to do the interviewing. Thus, what follows is the conversation Patrick wanted to have with Tyler, not the one you wanted to have.
Happily Patrick stayed true to the spirit of Conversations with Tyler, and their dialogue covers a wide range of topics including the the benefits of diverse monocultures, the state of macroeconomics, Donald Trump, the amazing economics faculty at GMU, Peter Thiel, Brian Eno, Thomas Schelling, why Twitter is underrated, and — most pressing of all — why Marginal Revolution is so strange looking.

very clear that in order to do really good work, there are a lot of "bench" skills one has
to acquire and there is a lot of actual specific knowledge. Any kind of life wasn't
designed with neat fundamental principles the way that maybe physics was. A lot of it is
obviously evolved and contingent and messy and complicated and all the rest. So there
is a lot of specific factual stuff to learn.
For those two reasons, there are very few
successful pure autodidacts in biology.
In virtually every case that I'm aware of, at
some point, you have to get direct experience in and with a top lab, where you're seeing
how people actually do it in practice. This also ties back to what we were discussing
previously: your question about the founders and what they learn from each other and so on.
There's an interesting book, Apprentice to Genius, that follows three generations of scientists.
So someone who mentored somebody else, who in turn mentored another scientist. And they're
all extremely successful. The book is this description of what they all did, but also this
reflection on: "What is it that was transferred?".
For example, one of the most important and subtle
questions in science is problem selection. How do you choose what to work on? No one tells
you what to do. And you do have to answer this question multiple times. With a company, in
some sense, you have to decide it once, and then it's an iterative process from there. Whereas in
science, you're frequently pursuing completely new problems. You need to choose something that's
sufficiently important and hard, so that it would be important if you succeeded, but it also
mustn't be so complex that progress becomes unachievable. This is what mentees learn
from their mentors according to the book.
Another thing the book talks about is learning
about high standards and what they actually are.
When I talk to people in other domains,
I hear very frequently, that when they worked with X person or Y organization or in Z
environment, they learned what great actually is.
And that just permanently changed their sense
for what their own standard for their work ought to be. So one version of what people
in their 20s should do is get some ideas for domains you're interested in, but then figure
out where can you learn the highest standards, where are the highest standards embodied, and
where can you go and experience that first hand.
Before we get back to Stripe and Arc Institute,
I want to touch on the Parker study for a second. There's a view that says: "If we
improve the NIH 10% or whatever percent, are we really making a dent in the fact that ideas
are getting harder to find over time? And how much of a difference do institutions make anyways? Is
it just about a number of researchers and how many people in society you can put into research? It's
not like Singapore can get a much more effective scientific institution that lets it compete with
America in science by following this approach.
What's wrong with that intuition?
Noah Smith and others have talked about, I can't remember the term he used, something
like “moneyism”. He had a funny phrase. It pertains to the presumption that there is
some constant elasticity between investment in some particular outcome, like building a
semiconductor factory in Arizona or a new bridge, and the outcome of the factory or the bridge.
First of all, the conversion rate between those inputs and the output is not a cosmological
constant. Maybe any of these things could be done for half or a tenth of the cost. Secondly, there
are even deeper questions as to, is it possible at all? What else would have to change for it to
be possible? What are the other constraints? By talking about these things in funding and
dollar terms, you're making the implicit assumption that the only relevant constraint
is the financial one, where in practice, maybe it's permits or labor shortages or other things.
In the context of the NIH and science and R&D, I'm really skeptical of this approach being
brought to bear, where we can just talk about the amount that we're spending on R&D and think that
that's implicitly a useful measure of the output.
To a fairly close approximation, there were
around 1% as many practicing professional scientists in the US pre-World War II as
there were post-World War II or say even 1950.
The other epiphenomena in papers or patents and so
forth tend to follow pretty similar ratios. We got a lot of pretty good stuff in the first half
of the century. Despite increasing the amount that we spend between two and maybe slightly more
than two orders of magnitude it's not clear to me that there is a direct linear relationship between
spending and output. When analyzing the NIH or how we should pursue any of this stuff, I try to get
more concrete and tactile and think, what would success here look like? What is happening today at
the microscale? What are the actual problems? What could success look like at the microscale?
What might it look like to scale that up?
One example of that, we ran a survey of the Fast
Grants grant recipients after Fast Grants asking about their normal work, and not about anything to
do with Fast Grants itself. We asked them if they had flexible funding, that is to say if they could
direct their current research dollars however they wanted how much their associated research program
would change. And we gave them three options: not much, a little, and a lot. Four out of
five(79%) said that their research agenda would change a lot if this constraint was removed.
Asking: "Should the NIH funding level be X or 1.1X or 1.2X or whatever?" seems to me like a bad
way to analyze this question as compared to: "How bound and constrained should an NIH grantee
be in choosing their research agenda?" Maybe their judgment is way better than that of the
committees, not saying it is, but who knows?
Maybe there's a 5X improvement to be generated
just by making that one switch? I'm very skeptical of these financially-oriented frameworks.
Maybe financial is not the right word for it, but just trying to map inputs to outputs
is the framing, which you're using to compare the pre-World War II inputs to what's happening
now. If it was particular to the scientific institutions, you'd expect, for example, that
things that are disconnected from the NIH-specific structures would show different trends. You've
talked a lot about the fact that it's getting harder to find impactful papers. Sector through
sector, it's not like NIH is running Moore's Law progress, right? But even there you see that you
need exponentially more researchers to keep up the same level of progress. It does seem important to
have these "level effects", that are one-time, in the case of something like COVID happens, where we
say: "Yeah, we need that level-effect" right now." But if we're framing it in terms of hundreds
of years from now, I wonder if these events are going to be a thing that increases growth
rates, which is a sort of framing that is also applied when talking about progress.
Does that make sense in that context, when all sectors are seeing slowdowns,
which seem consistent with how the economy and science progresses over time?
“I don't know” is the short answer.
It's really puzzling. The constancy of US
GDP growth is one of the weirdest things.
I don't know if I've got an explanation for it.
An obvious thing to do would be to shrug and say: "OK, well, it's overdetermined, and that's just
how countries work." But you can look at other countries, where it's manifestly not the case.
What is it that's weird and special about the US?
The thing that I wonder about in a lot of these
cases is: you could get many of the observed system phenomenon characteristics, if we weren't
actually adding productive capacity. That's a simple way to explain a lot of it, in that if
you're just adding exponentially more unproductive capacity, then on a stylized level, a lot of this
stuff would just fall out of it. Now, I'm not saying that we're necessarily doing that, but it
could be that maybe we're making them... There's lots of ways where that could be quite effectively
going on, even if it's not the case that the marginal people or things or organizations
themselves are bad. It's just how the components interact. But the fact that you could get these
exponentially diminishing returns through the addition of ever more nonproductive capacity
makes me not persuaded that the low-hanging case is necessarily true, and gives some
weight to the prospect that it's fundamentally structural, cultural, or organizational.
Just to give a micro example there, and it's a very basic and an obvious one, it's interesting

series together. Maybe we're just returning to the financial point again, but it seems pretty

Yeah, although the point about: "the marginal inputs we put into science have not been

The 1X then was a 1X of much higher quality, than
100X now. It's not clear what you do to fix that.
If it's just a case, that there's a limited

If the binding constraint is the number of John von Neumann's, then yes, that's bad news, I

Now, you might still say: "OK, fine,

OK, that's a great point to talk about, Arc institute. I think you just answered

Even though there are lots of scientists and lots of universities, there's a lot

Certainly, we're not claiming that it's a bad one.
Construct of universities, labs, PI — a principal

Homogeneity is bad in basically any ecosystem, especially ecosystems where you're producing
